## 3.2 Derivation 2: Annulus Area (Return Stroke / Rod-Side)

**Statement:** On the rod-side, the effective area is reduced by the area occupied by the piston rod.

**Step 1:** Full bore area is the circular area of the piston.

$$
A_{bore} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 2:** The piston rod has a smaller diameter $d$, occupying a circle of its own.

$$
A_{rod} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 3:** On the rod-side, air acts on the *annular* region (full circle minus the rod's circle).

$$
A_{a} = A_{bore} - A_{rod}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute and simplify.

$$
A_{a} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} - \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}
$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, return stroke force is:

$$
F_{ret} = P \times A_{a} = \dfrac{\pi P (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Piston Velocity and Air Consumption

**Step 1:** Air consumption per stroke is the cylinder's swept volume.

$$
V_{stroke} = A \times L
$$

**Step 2:** For a double-acting cylinder, air is consumed in *both* chambers per complete cycle. Use bore area on the cap end and annulus area on the rod end.

$$
V_{cycle} = A_{bore} \times L + A_{a} \times L = L \left( A_{bore} + A_{a} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** If the cylinder performs $n$ cycles per minute, air consumption per minute is:

$$
Q_{air} = \dfrac{V_{cycle} \times n}{1000} \quad \text{(in litres per minute)}
$$

**Step 4:** Piston velocity is volume flow rate divided by piston area.

$$
v = \dfrac{Q}{60 \times A}
$$

where $Q$ is in lpm, $A$ in mm², and $v$ in m/s (after unit conversion).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A double-acting cylinder has a bore $D = 50$ mm, rod diameter $d = 20$ mm, and stroke $L = 200$ mm. Supply pressure is $P = 5$ bar. The cylinder completes 30 cycles per minute. Calculate: (a) Forward thrust force, (b) Return thrust force, (c) Air consumption per minute in litres.

**Solution:**

**(a) Forward force (full bore):**

$$
A_{bore} = \dfrac{\pi (50)^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 2500}{4} = 1963.5 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
F_{fwd} = P \times A_{bore} = 5 \times 1963.5 = 9817.5 \text{ N}
$$

**(b) Return force (annulus):**

$$
A_{a} = \dfrac{\pi (50^{2} - 20^{2})}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (2500 - 400)}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 2100}{4} = 1649.3 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
F_{ret} = P \times A_{a} = 5 \times 1649.3 = 8246.7 \text{ N}
$$

**(c) Air consumption:**

$$
V_{cycle} = (A_{bore} + A_{a}) \times L = (1963.5 + 1649.3) \times 200 = 722{,}560 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

Convert to litres: $722{,}560 \text{ mm}^{3} \times 10^{-6} = 0.7226$ litres per cycle.

$$
Q_{air} = 0.7226 \times 30 = 21.68 \text{ lpm}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** This is a realistic figure — most small industrial pneumatic cylinders consume between 10 and 100 lpm at 5 bar.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Number of air ports | 1 | 2 |
| Force directions | Forward only (spring returns) | Forward and return |
| Air consumption | Lower (half) | Higher (both strokes) |
| Control valve | 3/2 valve | 5/2 (or 5/3) valve |
| Stroke length | Limited by spring length | Unlimited in theory |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical application | Clamping, ejection | Pushing, sorting, pressing |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Basic Pneumatic System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the cascade of energy and signal flow in a typical KTU lab pneumatic setup. Each block represents a functional unit and the arrows show the direction of air/signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEN["BLOCK 1: AIR GENERATION"]
        A1[MAINS SUPPLY]
        A2[COMPRESSOR]
        A3[RECEIVER TANK]
        A4[PRESSURE SWITCH]
    end

    subgraph CON["BLOCK 2: AIR CONDITIONING FRL"]
        B1[FILTER]
        B2[REGULATOR]
        B3[LUBRICATOR]
        B4[PRESSURE GAUGE]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["BLOCK 3: CONTROL AND DISTRIBUTION"]
        C1[3 BY 2 PUSH BUTTON VALVE]
        C2[5 BY 2 DIRECTION VALVE]
        C3[FLOW CONTROL VALVE]
        C4[QUICK EXHAUST VALVE]
    end

    subgraph ACT["BLOCK 4: ACTUATION AND WORK"]
        D1[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
        D2[LOAD ON PISTON]
        D3[WORKPIECE]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
    A4 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    B2 -.monitors.-> B4
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

## 4.2 Signal Sequence Chart for a Double-Acting Cylinder

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as OPERATOR
    participant V as 5 BY 2 VALVE
    participant C as CYLINDER
    participant LS as LIMIT SWITCH

    OP->>V: ACTUATE PUSH BUTTON
    V->>C: SUPPLY AIR TO CAP END
    C->>C: PISTON MOVES FORWARD
    C->>LS: TRIGGERS LIMIT SWITCH
    LS->>V: SHIFTS VALVE TO RETURN
    V->>C: SUPPLY AIR TO ROD END
    C->>C: PISTON RETRACTS
    C-->>OP: WORK COMPLETE
```

## 4.3 Component Symbol Reference (ISO 1219 / KTU Lab Standard)

| Component | ISO Symbol Description | Purpose |
|-----------|------------------------|---------|
| Compressor | Circle with arrow into tank | Air generation |
| FRL Unit | Filter (triangle), Regulator (square), Lubricator (drop) | Air conditioning |
| 3/2 DCV | Two squares with arrows and triangles | Push button / spring return |
| 5/2 DCV | Two squares with 5 ports and arrows | Direction control for DAC |
| Single-Acting Cylinder | Rectangle with one air port and spring symbol | Push only |
| Double-Acting Cylinder | Rectangle with two air ports | Push and pull |
| Flow Control Valve | Adjustable arrow with check valve | Throttle air in one direction |
| Silencer | Triangle with lines at end | Reduce exhaust noise |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Note:** In the actual experiment, the cylinder rod is usually connected to a small mechanical fixture (gripper, slider, or piston pushing a load cell) to demonstrate force or position.

## 4.4 Decision Flow for Selecting Cylinder Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{NEED FORCE IN BOTH DIRECTIONS?}
    Q1 -- YES --> X1[CHOOSE DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{IS STROKE LESS THAN 100 MM?}
    Q2 -- YES --> X2[CHOOSE SINGLE ACTING CYLINDER]
    Q2 -- NO --> X3[CHOOSE DOUBLE ACTING WITH EXTERNAL SPRING OR MECHANICAL RETURN]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a pneumatic system. List any four advantages of pneumatic systems over hydraulic systems.**

**Model Answer:**
A pneumatic system is a power transmission system that uses compressed air as the working fluid to generate, transmit, and control energy for performing mechanical work.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **Clean and non-flammable** — air is safe for food, pharmaceutical, and explosive environments.
2. **Lower cost of working fluid** — air is freely available from the atmosphere.
3. **Simple and compact components** — valves, cylinders, and fittings are lighter and cheaper.
4. **No overheating risk** — air can be vented directly to atmosphere; no return line needed.

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**State the function of the FRL unit in a pneumatic system. Why is the lubricator placed after the regulator?**

**Model Answer:**
The **FRL unit** performs three functions:
- **F (Filter):** Removes dust, dirt, and condensed moisture from compressed air.
- **R (Regulator):** Reduces and maintains a constant downstream working pressure.
- **L (Lubricator):** Adds a controlled amount of oil mist to the air for lubrication of moving parts.

**Why lubricator is placed after the regulator:** The regulator creates a small pressure drop across its diaphragm, which causes a Venturi effect downstream. This pressure drop draws oil up the suction tube and atomizes it into the air stream. If the lubricator were placed before the regulator, this pressure drop would not exist and oil would not be picked up.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the four basic functional blocks of a pneumatic system. State the role of each block. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A double-acting cylinder has a bore of 80 mm, a piston rod diameter of 25 mm, and a stroke of 250 mm. The supply pressure is 6 bar. Calculate: (i) Forward thrust force, (ii) Return thrust force, (iii) Air consumption per minute if the cylinder operates at 20 cycles per minute. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The four basic functional blocks of a pneumatic system are:

1. **Air Generation Block** — Compressor and receiver tank. The compressor converts electrical or mechanical energy into pneumatic energy by compressing atmospheric air. The receiver tank stores compressed air to maintain steady pressure.

2. **Air Conditioning Block (FRL Unit)** — Filter, Regulator, Lubricator. The filter removes contaminants; the regulator maintains constant working pressure; the lubricator adds oil mist for lubrication.

3. **Control and Distribution Block** — Direction control valves, flow control valves, push buttons, limit switches. These components route compressed air to the appropriate actuator port based on the desired logic.

4. **Actuation Block** — Cylinders (single-acting or double-acting) and motors. This block converts pneumatic energy back into useful mechanical work (linear or rotary motion).

> A neat block diagram with arrows showing air flow direction from compressor → FRL → valves → cylinder earns full marks.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $D = 80$ mm, $d = 25$ mm, $L = 250$ mm, $P = 6$ bar, $n = 20$ cycles/min.

**Step 1 — Compute geometric areas:**

$$
A_{bore} = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 80^{2}}{4} = 5026.55 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

$$
A_{a} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (80^{2} - 25^{2})}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (6400 - 625)}{4} = 4536.46 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert pressure to N/mm²:** $6 \text{ bar} = 0.6 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Forward thrust force (cap end):**

$$
F_{fwd} = P \times A_{bore} = 0.6 \times 5026.55 = 3015.93 \text{ N} \approx 3.02 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Return thrust force (rod end):**

$$
F_{ret} = P \times A_{a} = 0.6 \times 4536.46 = 2721.88 \text{ N} \approx 2.72 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Air consumption per cycle:**

$$
V_{cycle} = (A_{bore} + A_{a}) \times L = (5026.55 + 4536.46) \times 250 = 2{,}390{,}752.5 \text{ mm}^{3}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to litres and multiply by cycles:**

$$
V_{cycle} = 2.3907 \text{ litres}
$$

$$
Q_{air} = 2.3907 \times 20 = 47.81 \text{ litres per minute}
$$

**Final Answers:** $F_{fwd} = 3015.93$ N; $F_{ret} = 2721.88$ N; $Q_{air} = 47.81$ lpm.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a double-acting cylinder. Compare it with a single-acting cylinder in any four points. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A pneumatic system uses a single-acting cylinder of bore 40 mm and stroke 100 mm. The supply pressure is 5 bar and the spring constant is 4 N/mm. Calculate: (i) Theoretical forward force, (ii) Effective forward force (after spring force), (iii) Air consumption per minute if the cylinder cycles 40 times per minute. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction of a Double-Acting Cylinder:**
A double-acting cylinder consists of a cylindrical barrel with two end caps. A piston with seals is fitted inside the barrel. The piston is connected to a piston rod that extends out of the cylinder through a sealed gland. There are **two air ports** — one on the cap end and one on the rod end. End caps contain bearings to guide the rod and seals to prevent air leakage.

**Working:** When compressed air is admitted into the cap end, the piston moves forward (extension stroke). Simultaneously, the air from the rod end is exhausted to atmosphere. To retract the piston, the air supply is switched — air enters the rod end and the cap-end air is exhausted.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Single-Acting Cylinder | Double-Acting Cylinder |
|-----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Air ports | One | Two |
| Spring return | Yes (built-in) | No (air-powered in both directions) |
| Force direction | Forward only | Forward and return |
| Control valve | 3/2 valve | 5/2 or 5/3 valve |
| Air consumption | Lower | Higher |
| Stroke length | Limited (≤ 100 mm typically) | Practically unlimited |

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $D = 40$ mm, $L = 100$ mm, $P = 5$ bar, $k = 4$ N/mm, $n = 40$ cycles/min.

**Step 1 — Bore area:**

$$
A = \dfrac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 40^{2}}{4} = 1256.64 \text{ mm}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert pressure:** $5 \text{ bar} = 0.5 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$.

**Step 3 — Theoretical forward force:**

$$
F_{th} = P \times A = 0.5 \times 1256.64 = 628.32 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Spring force at full stroke:**

$$
F_{spring} = k \times L = 4 \times 100 = 400 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5 — Effective forward force:**

$$
F_{eff} = F_{th} - F_{spring} = 628.32 - 400 = 228.32 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 6 — Air consumption per cycle (single port only):**

$$
V_{cycle} = A \times L = 1256.64 \times 100 = 125{,}664 \text{ mm}^{3} = 0.1257 \text{ litres}
$$

**Step 7 — Air consumption per minute:**

$$
Q_{air} = 0.1257 \times 40 = 5.027 \text{ lpm}
$$

**Final Answers:** $F_{th} = 628.32$ N; $F_{eff} = 228.32$ N; $Q_{air} = 5.03$ lpm.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar to N/mm²** — pressure in KTU problems is often given in bar, but the area is in mm², so unit conversion is mandatory. Use $1 \text{ bar} = 0.1 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$.
> 2. **Confusing bore area with annulus area** — on the rod-side, students often use $\pi D^{2} / 4$ instead of $\pi (D^{2} - d^{2})/4$, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert mm³ to litres** — divide by $10^{6}$ (or multiply by $10^{-6}$).
> 4. **Spring force direction** — in single-acting cylinders, the spring *opposes* forward motion, so it is **subtracted**, not added.
> 5. **Units of spring constant** — $k$ in N/mm with $L$ in mm gives spring force in N. Do not mix with N/m or m units.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pneumatic System = Air as working fluid** for power transmission and automation.
- **Four functional blocks:** Generation → Conditioning (FRL) → Control (Valves) → Actuation (Cylinders/Motors).
- **FRL order is fixed:** Filter → Regulator → Lubricator (F-R-L).
- **Standard working pressure in KTU lab:** 4 – 6 bar.
- **Core formula:** $F = P \times A$ where $A = \pi D^{2} / 4$ (forward) or $A = \pi (D^{2} - d^{2})/4$ (return).
- **Pressure conversion:** 1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa = 0.1 N/mm².
- **Valve notation:** $n/m$ where $n$ = number of ports, $m$ = number of positions (e.g., 5/2, 3/2, 4/2).
- **Single-acting cylinder:** One air port + spring return → uses 3/2 valve.
- **Double-acting cylinder:** Two air ports, no spring → uses 5/2 (or 5/3) valve.
- **Air consumption:** Multiply cylinder swept volume by cycles per minute; convert mm³ to litres using $\div 10^{6}$.
- **Cylinder velocity:** $v = Q / A$ where $Q$ is volume flow rate and $A$ is piston area.
- **Mechanical efficiency** of a pneumatic cylinder is typically 80 – 95% (friction + leakage losses).
- **Why pneumatics over hydraulics:** clean, safe, cheaper, lighter, no return line, suitable for explosive and food-grade environments.
- **Why pneumatics inferior to hydraulics:** compressibility → poor stiffness, lower force density, audible exhaust noise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electro-Pneumatic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL507 - AUTOMATION LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Electro-Pneumatic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electro-Pneumatic System** is an integrated automation architecture in which **electrical control signals** (originating from switches, sensors, relays, or Programmable Logic Controllers - PLCs) are used to actuate **pneumatic power elements** (such as directional control valves, cylinders, and motors) to perform mechanical work. The system converts electrical energy into pneumatic energy via electromechanical transducers (solenoid coils) and uses compressed air as the working medium for linear or rotary motion.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCRUL507 - Automation Laboratory**, Module Experiment 2 specifically deals with the design, wiring, and troubleshooting of circuits that combine **electrical ladder logic** with **pneumatic circuit diagrams** to execute sequential operations on double-acting and single-acting cylinders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment focuses on implementing **sequential control of double-acting cylinders** using **5/2 Solenoid-Operated Directional Control Valves (DCVs)**, **3/2 roller/push-button valves**, **limit switches**, and standard pneumatic accessories (FRL unit, silencers).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **domestic washing machine**. When you press the "Start" button (an *electrical input*), a small electrical current flows to a solenoid valve. The solenoid opens a pathway, and pressurized water (the *pneumatic equivalent - compressed air*) rushes in to push a piston, which mechanically moves the drum agitator. The drum reaches a position, a sensor (a *limit switch*) detects it, sends an electrical signal back, which triggers another solenoid to reverse the airflow. The drum now moves the opposite way.

> This is the essence of an electro-pneumatic system:
> - **Brain (Electrical)** → decides what to do.
> - **Muscle (Pneumatic)** → does the heavy physical work.
> - **Nerves (Wires & Tubing)** → carry the signals and air.

In industrial automation, this principle is used in **bottling plants, packaging machines, car assembly lines, and CNC tool changers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU 2024):**
> - Standard Working Pressure: **6 bar (0.6 MPa / 87 psi)**
> - Standard Supply Voltage for Solenoids: **24 V DC**
> - Standard Cylinder Bore Sizes: **25 mm, 32 mm, 40 mm, 50 mm**
> - ISO 5599 / ISO 15407 Standard for valve mounting interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture Breakdown

An electro-pneumatic system can be functionally decomposed into the following structured layers:

- **I. Energy Supply Layer**
  - Compressor → produces compressed air at standard **6 bar**.
  - **FRL Unit (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)**: cleans, regulates pressure to working value, and lubricates air.
  - Manifold / Reservoir.

- **II. Signal/Input Layer (Electrical)**
  - **Push Buttons (NO/NC types)** - manual command initiation.
  - **Limit Switches (Roller, Lever, Whisker types)** - mechanical feedback.
  - **Pressure Switches** - fluidic feedback.
  - **Proximity Sensors (Inductive, Capacitive)** - non-contact feedback.
  - **Relays & Contactors** - signal amplification and logic.

- **III. Signal Processing Layer**
  - **Relay Logic** (traditional) or **PLC Ladder Logic** (modern KTU focus).
  - Logic functions: AND, OR, NOT, Memory (Latching).

- **IV. Actuation/Conversion Layer**
  - **Solenoid Coils** - convert electrical current into magnetic force.
  - **Directional Control Valves (DCVs)** - 3/2, 5/2, 5/3 configurations.
    - 3/2 = 3 ports, 2 positions
    - 5/2 = 5 ports, 2 positions (most common in KTU experiments)
  - The 5 ports are: **1 (Pressure), 2 & 4 (Outlets to cylinder), 3 & 5 (Exhausts)**.

- **V. Power Output Layer (Pneumatic)**
  - **Single-Acting Cylinder (SAC)**: spring return, one air port.
  - **Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)**: two air ports, power stroke both ways.
  - Pneumatic Motor (rotary application).

### 2.2 Operational "Why and How"

**Why use electro-pneumatic instead of pure pneumatic?**
- Pure pneumatic circuits become excessively complex beyond 4–5 cylinders due to cascade valve chains.
- Electrical signals travel at the **speed of light (~3 × 10⁸ m/s)**, while pneumatic signals travel at the **speed of sound in air (~343 m/s)**.
- Electrical signals can be processed logically (memory, timers, counters) with much higher reliability.

**How does the conversion happen?**
- When **24 V DC** is applied to a solenoid coil, current flows → magnetic flux is generated → flux pulls a ferromagnetic armature (plunger) against a spring → this mechanically shifts the valve spool → air pathways open or close.
- On de-energization, the spring returns the spool to its default position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Latch (Self-Holding) Logic** is the cornerstone of KTU experiments. A momentary push button (Start) energizes a relay; the relay's auxiliary NO contact closes in parallel with the button, "remembering" the ON state. A Stop (NC) button breaks the circuit.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates all governing equations for electro-pneumatic design calculations.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Symbol Description | Standard Unit |
|---|---------------------|---------|--------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Theoretical Cylinder Force | $F = P \times A$ | $F$ = Force, $P$ = Pressure, $A$ = Piston Area | $F$ [N], $P$ [Pa], $A$ [m²] |
| 2 | Piston Area (DAC) | $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | $D$ = Bore diameter | [m²] |
| 3 | Annulus Area (Rod Side) | $A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ | $d$ = Rod diameter | [m²] |
| 4 | Air Consumption per Stroke | $V_{stroke} = A \times L$ | $L$ = Stroke length | [m³] |
| 5 | Air Consumption per Minute | $Q = V_{stroke} \times n \times \dfrac{P_{work} + P_{atm}}{P_{atm}}$ | $n$ = Cycles/min | [m³/min] |
| 6 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $Q$ = Flow rate | [m/s] |
| 7 | Power Output | $P_{out} = F \times v$ | Mechanical power | [W] |
| 8 | Solenoid Pull-In Time | $t_{on} \approx 10 \text{ to } 30 \text{ ms}$ | Typical for 24 V DC | [ms] |
| 9 | Valve Response Time | $t_v = \dfrac{m_{spool}}{\Delta p \times A_{spool}}$ | $m$ = spool mass | [s] |
| 10 | Pressure Drop in Tube (Darcy) | $\Delta p = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Friction factor $f$ | [Pa] |
| 11 | Force Ratio (DAC extend vs retract) | $R = \dfrac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$ | Always greater than 1 | dimensionless |
| 12 | Spring Return Force | $F_s = k \times x$ | $k$ = stiffness, $x$ = compression | [N] |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** A real cylinder's effective force is **70–85%** of the theoretical value due to friction and back-pressure on the exhaust side. KTU problems typically ask for theoretical values unless otherwise specified.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cylinder Force Balance and the Extend-Retract Asymmetry

Consider a **Double-Acting Cylinder (DAC)** with bore $D$ and rod diameter $d$. Compressed air at gauge pressure $P_g$ is supplied.

**Step 1: Express piston area on the cap (blank) end during extension.**

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

This is the full circular area exposed to supply pressure during the **extension stroke**.

**Step 2: Express piston area on the rod end during extension.**

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$

The rod occupies central space, so the effective area is reduced by the cross-section of the rod.

**Step 3: Write the theoretical extending force.**

$$F_{ext} = P_g \times A_{cap} = P_g \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

**Step 4: Write the theoretical retracting force.**

During retraction, supply air enters the rod end and exhausts from the cap end.

$$F_{ret} = P_g \times A_{rod} = P_g \times \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$

**Step 5: Compute the Force Ratio $R$ to show asymmetry.**

$$R = \frac{F_{ext}}{F_{ret}} = \frac{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2}{\frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)} = \frac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$$

**Step 6: Numerical example for KTU problem solving.**

Let $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$, and $P_g = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.05)^2 - (0.025)^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0025 - 0.000625)$$

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.001875) = 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{ext} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} = 1178.1 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{ret} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 1.4726 \times 10^{-3} = 883.6 \text{ N}$$

$$R = \frac{1178.1}{883.6} \approx 1.333$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The cylinder pushes **33.3% harder** while extending than while retracting. This is critical when designing clamping circuits — designers often mount the workpiece on the **retract stroke** to ensure controlled force.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Air Consumption (Free Air Delivery Equivalent)

Compressed air is metered in two ways: as **free air** (at atmospheric pressure) or as **compressed air** (at working pressure). Pneumatic system sizing always requires the *free air* equivalent.

**Step 1: Compressed air volume per stroke.**

$$V_{comp} = A \times L$$

where $A$ is the effective piston area and $L$ is the stroke length.

**Step 2: Apply Boyle's Law for isothermal expansion back to atmospheric conditions.**

$$P_{work} \times V_{comp} = P_{atm} \times V_{free}$$

$$V_{free} = V_{comp} \times \frac{P_{work}}{P_{atm}}$$

(Where $P$ values are absolute, i.e., $P_{atm} = 1 \text{ bar}$, $P_{work} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.)

**Step 3: Multiply by cycle frequency to get volumetric flow rate.**

$$Q_{free} = V_{free} \times n = A \times L \times n \times \frac{P_{work}}{P_{atm}}$$

**Step 4: For a Double-Acting Cylinder, sum both chambers.**

$$Q_{DAC} = \left[ \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 L_{ext} + \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) L_{ret} \right] \times n \times \frac{P_{work}}{P_{atm}}$$

For a typical case where $L_{ext} = L_{ret} = L$:

$$Q_{DAC} = \frac{\pi}{4} L \, n \left( 2D^2 - d^2 \right) \frac{P_{work}}{P_{atm}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** This is the value to compare with the compressor's **Free Air Delivery (FAD)** rating to size the supply system correctly. A 100% safety margin is conventionally added ($Q_{compressor} \geq 2 \times Q_{demand}$).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU 2024 Problem Types

| Problem Type | Given Data | Required to Find | Key Equations Used |
|--------------|-----------|------------------|-------------------|
| Force on DAC at 6 bar | $D, d, P$ | $F_{ext}, F_{ret}$ | $F = P \times A$ |
| Cylinder Sizing for a Load | $m, \mu, P$ | Minimum $D$ | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4(F_{load} + \mu m g)}{\pi P}}$ |
| Cycle Time Calculation | $L, D, Q$ | Time per stroke | $t = \dfrac{A \times L}{Q}$ |
| Compressor Sizing | $n, L, D$ | Compressor FAD | $Q_{FAD} \geq 2 \times Q_{DAC}$ |
| Sequencing Time Delay | $S_1, S_2$ positions | Travel time | $t = \dfrac{S}{v} = \dfrac{S \times A}{Q}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Electro-Pneumatic System Architecture

The following diagram illustrates the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a complete electro-pneumatic system as studied in Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PWR1([AC Mains 230V 50Hz]) --> TR[Step Down Transformer 230V to 24V AC]
    TR --> REC[Bridge Rectifier]
    REC --> REG[DC Voltage Regulator 24V DC]
    REG --> BUS{DC Power Bus 24V}

    BUS --> IN1((Push Button START NO))
    BUS --> IN2((Push Button STOP NC))
    BUS --> IN3((Limit Switch LS1 Roller))
    BUS --> IN4((Limit Switch LS2 Roller))
    BUS --> IN5((Proximity Sensor P1))

    IN1 --> LOGIC[Relay Logic Ladder or PLC Module]
    IN2 --> LOGIC
    IN3 --> LOGIC
    IN4 --> LOGIC
    IN5 --> LOGIC

    LOGIC --> R1[Relay R1 Coil 24V DC]
    LOGIC --> R2[Relay R2 Coil 24V DC]
    LOGIC --> SOL1((Solenoid Y1 of 5 by 2 DCV))
    LOGIC --> SOL2((Solenoid Y2 of 5 by 2 DCV))

    R1 --> CONT1[Contact set R1 NO in holding branch]
    R1 --> CONT2[Contact set R1 NO to drive SOL1]

    COMP([Compressor 6 bar]) --> TANK[Air Receiver Tank]
    TANK --> FRL[FRL Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    FRL --> MFD[Manifold Pressure 6 bar]

    MFD --> VALVE1{5 by 2 DCV Solenoid Pilot Operated Spring Return}
    SOL1 --> VALVE1
    SOL2 --> VALVE1

    VALVE1 -- Port 2 --> CYL1[Double Acting Cylinder DAC A Cap End]
    VALVE1 -- Port 4 --> CYL2[Double Acting Cylinder DAC A Rod End]
    CYL1 --> PISTON1((Piston with rod))
    CYL2 --> PISTON1
    PISTON1 --> MECH[Mechanical Output Linear Motion]

    MFD --> VALVE2{5 by 2 DCV for Cylinder B}
    VALVE2 -- Port 2 --> DACY1[Cap End of DAC B]
    VALVE2 -- Port 4 --> DACY2[Rod End of DAC B]
    DACY1 --> PISTON2((Piston B))
    DACY2 --> PISTON2
    PISTON2 --> MECH2[Workpiece Transfer Action]

    IN3 -. Feedback Signal .-> LOGIC
    IN4 -. Feedback Signal .-> LOGIC
    IN5 -. Feedback Signal .-> LOGIC

    VALVE1 --> EXH1((Silencer Port 3))
    VALVE1 --> EXH2((Silencer Port 5))
    VALVE2 --> EXH3((Silencer Port 3))
    VALVE2 --> EXH4((Silencer Port 5))

    MFD --> PG[Pressure Gauge 0 to 10 bar]
    FRL --> PR[Pressure Regulator Knob 0 to 8 bar]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: A+B+ A− B− Step Sequence (Classic KTU Problem)

The following sequence diagram represents the canonical "A extends, then B extends, then A retracts, then B retracts" cycle, the most commonly asked KTU Module 2 experiment.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant OP as Operator Push Button START
    participant LOG as Ladder Logic Memory Latch
    participant V1 as Solenoid Y1 of 5/2 Valve A
    participant CYLA as Cylinder A Double Acting
    participant LS1 as Limit Switch 1A Extended
    participant V2 as Solenoid Y2 of 5/2 Valve B
    participant CYLB as Cylinder B Double Acting
    participant LS2 as Limit Switch 2B Extended
    participant V1R as Solenoid Y1R of Valve A Retract
    participant V2R as Solenoid Y2R of Valve B Retract
    participant LS3 as Limit Switch 1A Retracted
    participant LS4 as Limit Switch 2B Retracted

    OP->>LOG: Pulse ON signal
    LOG->>V1: Energize Y1
    V1->>CYLA: Air to Port 2 Cap End
    CYLA->>LS1: Piston reaches end of stroke
    LS1->>LOG: Feedback to extend B
    LOG->>V2: Energize Y2
    V2->>CYLB: Air to Port 2 Cap End
    CYLB->>LS2: Piston B reaches end of stroke
    LS2->>LOG: Feedback to retract A
    LOG->>V1R: De energize Y1 Energize Y1R
    V1R->>CYLA: Air to Port 4 Rod End
    CYLA->>LS3: Piston A returns home
    LS3->>LOG: Feedback to retract B
    LOG->>V2R: De energize Y2 Energize Y2R
    V2R->>CYLB: Air to Port 4 Rod End
    CYLB->>LS4: Piston B returns home
    LS4->>LOG: Cycle complete Reset ready for next START
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Note:** The numbering of ports in a 5/2 valve is standardized as: **1 = Pressure Inlet, 2 = Outlet A (cap end), 4 = Outlet B (rod end), 3 and 5 = Exhausts**. Memorizing this mapping is essential for drawing correct ISO 1219 circuit symbols.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define an electro-pneumatic system. List any four components used in such a system.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An electro-pneumatic system is a control system in which electrical signals are used to operate pneumatic actuators, with compressed air performing the mechanical work.
The four essential components are:
1. **5/2 Solenoid-Operated Directional Control Valve** (signal-to-pneumatic converter).
2. **Double-Acting Cylinder** (pneumatic actuator).
3. **Limit Switch** (electrical feedback sensor).
4. **Relay or PLC** (signal processing element).
*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing 4 components + 1 mark for correct categorization)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
State the function of an FRL unit. What standard working pressure is used in KTU laboratory pneumatic trainers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator)** unit performs three functions:
- **Filter:** Removes dust, moisture, and contaminants from compressed air.
- **Regulator:** Maintains a constant downstream pressure regardless of supply fluctuations.
- **Lubricator:** Injects a fine mist of oil into the airstream to reduce friction inside valves and cylinders.

The standard working pressure used in KTU laboratory pneumatic trainers is **6 bar (0.6 MPa)**.
*(1 mark FRL purpose + 1 mark each for 2 functions elaborated + 1 mark for pressure value)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Pattern:** Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **5/2 solenoid-operated, spring-return, pilot-actuated DCV**. List the function of each of its five ports. (7 marks)

**(b)** A double-acting cylinder has a bore of 63 mm and a rod diameter of 25 mm. The supply pressure is 6 bar. The cylinder extends against a constant load of 800 N. Calculate (i) the extending force, (ii) the retracting force, and (iii) the percentage difference in force. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Construction and Working of 5/2 Solenoid-Operated Spring-Return DCV (7 marks):**

A 5/2 DCV has **5 ports** and **2 positions**. Construction consists of:

- **Body:** Aluminum alloy housing with five machined ports.
- **Spool:** A precision-ground cylindrical steel spool with lands and grooves.
- **Solenoid Coil (Y1):** Energized by 24 V DC, generates a magnetic field.
- **Pilot Ports:** Small air passages that channel air to shift the main spool.
- **Return Spring:** Pushes the spool back to the default position when the solenoid is de-energized.

**Working Principle:**

- **De-energized (Normal) State:** Spring keeps the spool in the right-hand position. Port 1 is connected to Port 2, sending air to the cap end. Ports 4 and 5 are linked, exhausting the rod-end air.
- **Energized State:** Current through coil Y1 creates a magnetic flux that overcomes the spring force. The pilot air then pushes the main spool leftward. Port 1 now connects to Port 4, sending air to the rod end. Ports 2 and 3 are linked, exhausting the cap end.

| Port Number | Function |
|-------------|----------|
| 1 | Pressure Inlet (from FRL) |
| 2 | Outlet to Cap End of Cylinder |
| 3 | Exhaust from Cap End (silenced) |
| 4 | Outlet to Rod End of Cylinder |
| 5 | Exhaust from Rod End (silenced) |

*(1 mark for sketch label + 2 marks for construction + 2 marks for working + 2 marks for port table = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Bore $D = 63 \text{ mm} = 0.063 \text{ m}$
- Rod diameter $d = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- Supply pressure $P = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- Load $F_{load} = 800 \text{ N}$

**Step 1: Compute cap-end area.**

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.063)^2 = 3.1172 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute rod-end area.**

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} ((0.063)^2 - (0.025)^2)$$

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.003969 - 0.000625) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.003344)$$

$$A_{rod} = 2.6265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3: Compute extending force.**

$$F_{ext} = P \times A_{cap} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 3.1172 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext} = 1870.3 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 4: Compute retracting force.**

$$F_{ret} = P \times A_{rod} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 2.6265 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret} = 1575.9 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5: Percentage difference.**

$$\% \text{ Diff} = \frac{F_{ext} - F_{ret}}{F_{ext}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Diff} = \frac{1870.3 - 1575.9}{1870.3} \times 100 = \frac{294.4}{1870.3} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Diff} \approx 15.74\%}$$

**Verification:** Since $F_{ext} = 1870.3 \text{ N} > 800 \text{ N}$ (load), the cylinder can perform the extension work safely. The retracting force of 1575.9 N is also well above the load, ensuring positive return. *(1 mark area + 1 mark force calc + 1 mark retracting + 2 marks numerical sub-results + 1 mark verification + 1 mark commentary = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Design an electro-pneumatic circuit for the following sequence using two double-acting cylinders **A and B**: **A+ B+ B− A−**. Draw the pneumatic circuit and the corresponding electrical ladder diagram. State any two assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pneumatic system requires a cylinder of bore 50 mm and stroke 200 mm to operate at 60 cycles per minute. The supply pressure is 6 bar. Calculate the free air consumption in liters per minute. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Circuit Design for A+ B+ B− A− Sequence (7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Cylinders A and B are double-acting with spring-centered 5/3 valves (or two 5/2 solenoid valves each).
2. A single Start push button (NO, momentary) initiates the sequence; the cycle auto-resets via limit switch feedback.

**Operation Logic:**
- **Step 1 (A+):** Energize solenoid Y1 of valve A → A extends → triggers limit switch 1a (extended).
- **Step 2 (B+):** Limit switch 1a energizes solenoid Y3 of valve B → B extends → triggers limit switch 2b (extended).
- **Step 3 (B−):** Limit switch 2b energizes solenoid Y4 of valve B → B retracts → triggers limit switch 2a (retracted).
- **Step 4 (A−):** Limit switch 2a energizes solenoid Y2 of valve A → A retracts → triggers limit switch 1b (retracted).
- Limit switch 1b resets the memory latch; ready for next START.

**Ladder Diagram Rungs:**

| Rung | Logic Expression | Action |
|------|-----------------|--------|
| 1 | $START \cdot 1b \rightarrow R_M$ (Memory) | Latch master relay |
| 2 | $R_M \cdot \overline{1a} \rightarrow Y1$ | Extend A |
| 3 | $1a \cdot \overline{2b} \rightarrow Y3$ | Extend B |
| 4 | $2b \cdot \overline{2a} \rightarrow Y4$ | Retract B |
| 5 | $2a \cdot \overline{1b} \rightarrow Y2$ | Retract A |
| 6 | $STOP$ (NC) in series with Rung 1 | Emergency stop |

*(2 marks assumptions + 2 marks sequence explanation + 3 marks ladder logic = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Free Air Consumption Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Bore $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- Stroke $L = 200 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$
- Cycles $n = 60 \text{ cycles/min}$
- Gauge pressure $P_g = 6 \text{ bar}$, so absolute $P_{work} = 7 \text{ bar} = 7 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_{atm} = 1 \text{ bar} = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 1: Compute cap-end area.**

$$A_{cap} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Assuming rod diameter is not given, treat this as a single-acting volume problem on cap end. (KTU problems often simplify this.)

**Step 2: Compressed air volume per stroke.**

$$V_{comp} = A_{cap} \times L = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 3: Free air volume per stroke (Boyle's Law).**

$$V_{free} = V_{comp} \times \frac{P_{work}}{P_{atm}} = 3.927 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{7}{1}$$

$$V_{free} = 2.749 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \text{ per stroke}$$

**Step 4: Multiply by cycles per minute.**

$$Q_{free} = V_{free} \times n = 2.749 \times 10^{-3} \times 60$$

$$Q_{free} = 0.1649 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 5: Convert to liters.**

$$\boxed{Q_{free} = 164.94 \text{ L/min}}$$

**Compressor Sizing:** With a 100% safety factor, select a compressor with FAD ≥ 330 L/min.

*(1 mark area + 1 mark compressed volume + 2 marks Boyle's Law application + 1 mark multiplication + 1 mark conversion + 1 mark commentary = 7 marks)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Port Numbering Error:** Students frequently label valve ports as 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 sequentially along the body. The correct ISO 1219 standard is **1-2-3-4-5** (P-A-B-T1-T2). Using sequential numbering (1-2-3-4-5 in flow order) results in **−1 to −2 marks** deduction in circuit questions.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Absolute vs Gauge Pressure:** In free air consumption questions, students often use $P = 6 \text{ bar}$ (gauge) directly. The correct value in Boyle's Law must be **absolute**, i.e., $P_{abs} = 6 + 1 = 7 \text{ bar}$. This single error costs **2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing Single-Acting vs Double-Acting Air Consumption:** For DAC, you must sum the cap-end and rod-end volumes per cycle. For SAC, only the active chamber is consumed. Mixing these up forfeits **1.5 marks**.
>
> 4. **Ladder Logic Without Memory (Latching):** If your sequence design omits a self-holding contact, you will lose the entire **3-mark logic portion** because the sequence will not be repeatable.
>
> 5. **Units Conversion Slip:** Mixing mm² with m² or bar with Pa in the same calculation leads to errors of magnitude $10^{10}$. Always convert to SI base units **first**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Electro-Pneumatic Systems:**
>
> - **5/2 DCV ports:** 1 (P), 2 (A), 3 (T_A), 4 (B), 5 (T_B). Memorize cold.
> - **DAC force ratio:** $F_{extend} > F_{retract}$ by factor $\dfrac{D^2}{D^2 - d^2}$.
> - **Standard KTU lab values:** 6 bar pressure, 24 V DC solenoids, double-acting cylinders.
> - **Boyle's Law requirement:** Always use **absolute pressure** in free air calculations.
> - **Ladder logic essentials:** Latching (memory), limit switch feedback, NC stop button in series with master rung.
> - **FRL sequence in supply line:** Compressor → Tank → Filter → Regulator → Lubricator → Manifold → Valves.
> - **Solenoid response time:** ~10–30 ms; pneumatic cylinder speed depends on flow control valves.
> - **Force equation:** $F = P \times A$; remember to subtract friction (10–15% in real systems).
> - **Common sequences tested:** A+B+, A+B+B−A−, A+B−A−B+, A+B+A−B−.
> - **Cascading vs PLC:** KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes both relay logic (traditional) and PLC (modern). Be prepared to draw both.
> - **Safety:** Always depressurize the system before changing connections; use silencers on exhaust ports to reduce noise below 85 dB.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Hydraulic Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL507 - AUTOMATION LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Hydraulic Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

A **Hydraulic System** is a power transmission system that uses a pressurised incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil) as the working medium to transmit energy from one location to another, performing mechanical work through the conversion of fluid pressure into linear or rotary motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A hydraulic system is an arrangement of interconnected components that uses the principle of **Pascal's Law** to multiply force. The fluid (hydraulic oil) is circulated through the system by a pump, controlled by valves, and delivered to actuators (cylinders or motors) to perform useful mechanical work.

## Pascal's Law — The Heart of Hydraulics

> **Pascal's Law (KTU High-Yield Principle):** *"Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions, and acts with equal force on equal areas."*

Mathematically expressed as:

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

where:
- $P$ = Pressure (Pa or N/m²)
- $F$ = Applied Force (N)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional Area (m²)

## Real-World Analogy — Making It Intuitive 💡

Imagine you are squeezing a **toothpaste tube** from the middle. The paste inside flows out equally from **both ends** of the nozzle. This is Pascal's Law in action — pressure is transmitted throughout the fluid.

Now scale this up:
- 🚗 **Car Hydraulic Brakes**: A small force on the brake pedal (small piston) creates massive braking force on the wheel cylinders (large piston).
- 🏗️ **Hydraulic Jack**: A garage jack lifts a 2-tonne car using just one hand, because a small piston pushes oil into a larger cylinder.
- 🚜 **JCB / Excavator**: A compact engine drives a small pump, but the hydraulic cylinders lift multi-tonne loads — this is **force multiplication** in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** *Small area + small force = Large area + LARGE force* (Pressure stays the same; only force and area change).

## Standard Metrics in Hydraulic Systems

| Parameter | Standard Unit | Conversion to SI |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Pressure | **bar** | 1 bar = **10⁵ Pa** = **100 kPa** |
| Flow rate | L/min (lpm) | 1 L/min = **1.667 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s** |
| Force | kN (kiloNewton) | 1 kN = **1000 N** |
| Power | kW (kilowatt) | 1 HP = **0.7457 kW** |
| Viscosity | cSt (centiStokes) | 1 cSt = **10⁻⁶ m²/s** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Components of a Basic Hydraulic System

A hydraulic system is composed of **four functional groups**:

### (i) Prime Mover & Power Supply
- **Electric Motor / Diesel Engine**: Provides mechanical input energy to the pump.
- **Reservoir (Tank)**: Stores hydraulic oil, allows air/heat separation, and provides settling space for contaminants.

### (ii) Energy Conversion Devices
- **Hydraulic Pump**: Converts mechanical energy → hydraulic energy (pressure × flow). Types: Gear pump, Vane pump, Piston pump.
- **Hydraulic Motor**: Converts hydraulic energy → rotary mechanical energy (opposite of pump).
- **Hydraulic Cylinder**: Converts hydraulic energy → linear mechanical force and motion.

### (iii) Control Elements
- **Directional Control Valve (DCV)**: Controls the direction of fluid flow (4/2, 4/3 valves).
- **Pressure Control Valve**: Includes Pressure Relief Valve (PRV), Pressure Reducing Valve.
- **Flow Control Valve**: Regulates flow rate (and thus speed) — Meter-in, Meter-out, Bleed-off.

### (iv) Conditioning & Auxiliary Elements
- **Filter**: Removes contaminants (typically 10–25 microns).
- **Accumulator**: Stores hydraulic energy as pressurised fluid (gas-charged bladder type).
- **Pressure Gauge & Flow Meter**: For monitoring system parameters.
- **Heat Exchanger**: Maintains oil temperature (ideal: 40–60 °C).

## 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Energy Input**: Electric motor drives the pump mechanically.
2. **Pressurisation**: Pump draws oil from the reservoir and pressurises it (typically 70–200 bar).
3. **Distribution**: Pressurised oil flows through pipelines to the directional control valve.
4. **Direction Control**: The DCV routes the oil to either the cap end or rod end of the cylinder (extension/retraction).
5. **Actuation**: Oil enters the cylinder, pushes the piston, and produces linear force.
6. **Return**: Exhaust oil returns to the reservoir through a filter.
7. **Safety**: The Pressure Relief Valve (PRV) opens at set pressure, returning excess oil to the tank.
8. **Speed Control**: A flow control valve regulates cylinder speed by restricting flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why force multiplication works:** Since $P$ is constant throughout the fluid, $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$. If $A_2 = 10 \times A_1$, then $F_2 = 10 \times F_1$. The pump and the small piston do small work, but the large piston does huge work.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Gold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas account for nearly 90% of numerical questions in KTU Board Exams on Basic Hydraulics. Master this table.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Pressure** | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $P$ in Pa, $F$ in N, $A$ in m² |
| 2 | **Force on Piston** | $F = P \times A$ | Output force in N |
| 3 | **Cylinder Bore Area (Extension)** | $A_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | $D$ = Piston diameter (m) |
| 4 | **Annular Area (Retraction)** | $A_{ret} = \dfrac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$ | $d$ = Rod diameter (m) |
| 5 | **Flow Rate** | $Q = A \times v$ | $Q$ in m³/s, $v$ in m/s |
| 6 | **Flow Conversion** | $Q_{(L/min)} = 60000 \times Q_{(m^3/s)}$ | Conversion factor |
| 7 | **Cylinder Velocity** | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | $v$ in m/s |
| 8 | **Hydraulic Power** | $P_{hyd} = P \times Q$ | $P$ in W if $P$ in Pa, $Q$ in m³/s |
| 9 | **Power in kW (Practical)** | $P_{(kW)} = \dfrac{P_{(bar)} \times Q_{(L/min)}}{600}$ | Quick exam formula |
| 10 | **Pump Volumetric Efficiency** | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Expressed in % |
| 11 | **Mechanical Advantage** | $MA = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{F_1}$ | Ratio (dimensionless) |
| 12 | **Pressure Conversion** | $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$ | SI conversion |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing**: Hydraulic presses (forging, stamping, injection moulding machines) — forces up to **50,000 kN**.
- **Construction**: Excavators, cranes, bulldozers, concrete pumps.
- **Automotive**: Power steering, braking systems, suspensions, lift platforms.
- **Aerospace**: Flight control actuators, landing gear systems.
- **Marine**: Steering gear, deck cranes, hatch covers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Hydraulic Cylinder Force (Extension)

### Statement
A hydraulic cylinder with piston diameter $D$ is supplied with oil at pressure $P$. Derive the expression for the extension force exerted by the piston.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Pascal's Law to the fluid in contact with the piston.**

By Pascal's Law, the pressure acting on the piston face is uniform and equal to the supply pressure.

$$P = \frac{F}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Express the area of the piston (a circle of diameter $D$).**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $A$ into the pressure equation.**

$$P = \frac{F}{\frac{\pi}{4} D^2}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to obtain Force.**

$$F = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

**Step 5 — Final boxed result (the KTU standard formula).**

$$\boxed{F_{extension} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times P \times D^2}$$

This force pushes the piston outwards, extending the cylinder rod.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydraulic Cylinder Force (Retraction)

During retraction, oil enters the **annular region** (between piston and rod), since the rod side is blocked. The effective area reduces.

$$A_{ret} = A_{piston} - A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 - \frac{\pi}{4} d^2$$

$$\boxed{F_{retraction} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times P \times (D^2 - d^2)}$$

where $d$ is the piston rod diameter.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cylinder Extension Velocity

The volumetric flow rate entering the cylinder fills the extension volume at a rate determined by piston speed.

**Step 1 — Continuity equation for incompressible fluid.**

$$Q = A \times v$$

**Step 2 — Express area in terms of bore diameter $D$.**

$$Q = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \times v$$

**Step 3 — Solve for velocity $v$.**

$$v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q$ in L/min and $D$ in mm for the exam-friendly form.**

$$v_{(m/s)} = \frac{Q_{(L/min)} \times 10^{-3}/60}{\frac{\pi}{4} (D \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

Simplified practical formula:

$$\boxed{v_{(m/s)} = \frac{Q_{(L/min)}}{15 \times \pi \times D^2_{(cm)}}}$$

For KTU exams, use the SI method step-by-step and keep units consistent.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hydraulic Power Delivered to the Actuator

**Step 1 — Energy per unit volume in a pressurised fluid.**

The work done by pressure $P$ in displacing volume $V$ is:

$$W = P \times V$$

**Step 2 — Power is the rate of doing work.**

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{dW}{dt} = P \times \frac{dV}{dt} = P \times Q$$

**Step 3 — Result in SI units (W).**

$$P_{hyd}\,(\text{W}) = P\,(\text{Pa}) \times Q\,(\text{m}^3/\text{s})$$

**Step 4 — Result in practical engineering units (kW).**

Convert $P$ in bar to Pa (×10⁵) and $Q$ in L/min to m³/s (×10⁻³/60):

$$P_{hyd}\,(\text{kW}) = \frac{P\,(\text{bar}) \times Q\,(\text{L/min})}{600}$$

**Step 5 — Including overall efficiency $\eta$ of the system.**

$$\boxed{P_{input} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{P \times Q}{600 \times \eta}}$$

---

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 100 mm and rod diameter of 60 mm. The pump supplies oil at 80 bar pressure and 30 L/min flow rate. Calculate:
- (a) Extension force
- (b) Retraction force
- (c) Extension velocity
- (d) Hydraulic power in kW

**Given Data:**
- $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$
- $P = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = 30 \times 10^{-3} / 60 = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

### Part (a): Extension Force

$$A_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{ext} = P \times A_{ext} = 80 \times 10^5 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 62{,}832 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext} \approx 62.83 \text{ kN}}$$

### Part (b): Retraction Force

$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.1^2 - 0.06^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.01 - 0.0036) = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{ret} = P \times A_{ret} = 80 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 40{,}212 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret} \approx 40.21 \text{ kN}}$$

### Part (c): Extension Velocity

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{ext}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0637 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{ext} \approx 63.66 \text{ mm/s}}$$

### Part (d): Hydraulic Power

$$P_{hyd} = P \times Q = 80 \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 4000 \text{ W} = 4 \text{ kW}$$

Or using the quick formula:

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{80 \times 30}{600} = 4 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = 4 \text{ kW}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Basic Hydraulic System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the functional flow of energy and fluid through a basic hydraulic system, mapping every standard component encountered in a KTU laboratory setup.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MTR[Electric Motor] --> PUMP[Hydraulic Pump]
    TANK[Oil Reservoir Tank] --> PUMP
    PUMP --> PRV[Pressure Relief Valve]
    PRV --> DCV[4 by 3 Directional Control Valve]
    DCV --> FCV[Flow Control Valve]
    FCV --> CYL[Double Acting Cylinder]
    CYL --> DCV
    DCV --> FLT[Return Line Filter]
    FLT --> TANK
    ACC[Accumulator] -.-> DCV
    PG[Pressure Gauge] -.-> DCV
    FM[Flow Meter] -.-> FCV
```

### Description of Flow (Reading the Diagram)

- The **Electric Motor** mechanically drives the **Hydraulic Pump**.
- The pump draws filtered oil from the **Reservoir** and delivers pressurised oil to the system.
- The **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** protects the system from over-pressure (set at 110% of working pressure).
- The **4/3 Directional Control Valve** routes oil to the cap-end (extension) or rod-end (retraction) of the cylinder.
- The **Flow Control Valve** regulates cylinder extension/retraction speed.
- Exhaust oil returns through the **Return Line Filter** to the reservoir.
- **Pressure Gauge** and **Flow Meter** provide real-time monitoring.
- The **Accumulator** stores energy and smooths pressure pulsations.

---

## 4.2 Standard Hydraulic Circuit — Double-Acting Cylinder with Meter-Out Speed Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Power Supply Unit]
        T1[Reservoir] --> P1[Fixed Displacement Pump]
        M1[Electric Motor] --> P1
    end
    P1 --> V1[Pressure Relief Valve]
    V1 --> DCV1[4 by 3 DCV Centre Closed Position]
    DCV1 --> FCV1[Meter Out Flow Control Valve]
    FCV1 --> CYL1[Cylinder Cap End Port A]
    DCV1 --> CYL2[Cylinder Rod End Port B]
    CYL1 --> RET[Return Line]
    CYL2 --> RET
    RET --> T1
    DCV1 --> PG1[Pressure Gauge 0 to 200 bar]
```

### Working of the Circuit

- **Pump Centre Position (Neutral)**: DCV in centre position blocks flow → pump unloads to tank (or centre closed holds cylinder).
- **DCV in Left Position**: Pump flow goes to **Port A (cap end)** → piston extends → oil from rod end returns to tank via Port B.
- **DCV in Right Position**: Pump flow goes to **Port B (rod end)** → piston retracts → oil from cap end returns to tank via Port A.
- **Meter-Out Valve**: Installed on the return line, throttles exhaust oil → provides smooth, controlled retraction (avoids cavitation).

---

## 4.3 Comparison Block: Key Hydraulic Symbols Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reservoir] --> A1[Open or Pressurised Tank]
    B[Pump Symbol] --> B1[Fixed or Variable Displacement]
    C[DCV Symbol] --> C1[4 by 2 or 4 by 3 Directional]
    D[PRV Symbol] --> D1[Direct or Pilot Operated Relief]
    E[FCV Symbol] --> E1[Meter In or Meter Out or Bleed Off]
    F[Cylinder] --> F1[Single or Double Acting]
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU laboratory exams, students are often asked to **identify components from a given circuit diagram**. Memorise the standard ISO 1219 symbols for: Pump (circle with arrow), DCV (squares with arrows), PRV (spring-loaded arrow), and Cylinder (rectangle with piston rod).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. State Pascal's Law. How is it applied in a hydraulic press? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Pascal's Law:** *"Pressure applied at any point in a confined incompressible fluid at rest is transmitted equally and undiminished in all directions throughout the fluid."*

**Application in Hydraulic Press:**
A hydraulic press consists of two cylinders of different cross-sectional areas ($A_1$ small, $A_2$ large) connected at the base and filled with incompressible oil.

When a small force $F_1$ is applied on the small piston, pressure $P = F_1/A_1$ is developed. This same pressure acts on the large piston, producing an output force:

$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = F_1 \times \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

Since $A_2 \gg A_1$, the output force is enormously amplified, allowing the press to deform metals, form components, etc.

*(Mark Distribution: Law statement – 1.5 marks; Hydraulic press explanation with formula – 1.5 marks)*

---

### Q2. List any six major components of a hydraulic system and state the function of each. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Component | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Reservoir** | Stores hydraulic oil, dissipates heat, separates air and contaminants |
| 2 | **Hydraulic Pump** | Converts mechanical energy into hydraulic (pressure × flow) energy |
| 3 | **Directional Control Valve (DCV)** | Routes fluid to desired actuator port (extend/retract/neutral) |
| 4 | **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** | Limits maximum system pressure; protects components from over-pressure |
| 5 | **Flow Control Valve (FCV)** | Regulates flow rate and hence speed of actuator |
| 6 | **Hydraulic Cylinder** | Converts hydraulic energy into linear mechanical force and motion |

*(1 mark per correct entry × 2 entries in detail = 3 marks)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Pattern: Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a basic hydraulic system. List the major components and explain the function of the pressure relief valve and directional control valve. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A basic hydraulic system converts mechanical energy into hydraulic energy and back into mechanical work using an incompressible fluid.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Motor] --> P1[Pump]
    T1[Reservoir] --> P1
    P1 --> PRV1[Pressure Relief Valve]
    PRV1 --> DCV1[Directional Control Valve]
    DCV1 --> FCV1[Flow Control Valve]
    FCV1 --> CYL1[Hydraulic Cylinder]
    CYL1 --> T1
```

**Working:**
1. The **electric motor** drives the **hydraulic pump**, which draws oil from the reservoir.
2. The pump delivers pressurised oil (typically 70–200 bar) to the **Directional Control Valve (DCV)**.
3. The DCV routes the oil to the desired port of the **hydraulic cylinder**, causing extension or retraction.
4. Exhaust oil returns to the reservoir through a filter.
5. The **Flow Control Valve** regulates the actuator speed.
6. The **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** ensures safety by venting excess oil to the tank if pressure exceeds the set value.
7. The cylinder produces **linear mechanical force** to perform useful work (lifting, pushing, pressing).

**Function of PRV:** The PRV is normally closed. When system pressure exceeds the spring setting, the valve opens and dumps excess oil back to the tank, maintaining safe operating pressure.

**Function of DCV:** The DCV controls the direction of fluid flow. A 4/3 DCV has 4 ports and 3 positions, allowing extension, retraction, and a neutral (centre) position.

*(Diagram – 2 marks; Working steps – 2 marks; PRV function – 1.5 marks; DCV function – 1.5 marks)*

---

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter of 80 mm and a piston rod diameter of 50 mm. The pump supplies oil at a pressure of 100 bar and a flow rate of 40 L/min. Calculate: **(i)** The extension force, **(ii)** The retraction force, **(iii)** The extension velocity, and **(iv)** The hydraulic power developed. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$
- $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $P = 100 \text{ bar} = 100 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1: Areas**

$$A_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08^2 - 0.05^2) = 3.063 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 (i): Extension Force**

$$F_{ext} = P \times A_{ext} = 100 \times 10^5 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} = 50{,}270 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ext} = 50.27 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 (ii): Retraction Force**

$$F_{ret} = P \times A_{ret} = 100 \times 10^5 \times 3.063 \times 10^{-3} = 30{,}630 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret} = 30.63 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 (iii): Extension Velocity**

$$v_{ext} = \frac{Q}{A_{ext}} = \frac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{ext} = 132.6 \text{ mm/s}}$$

**Step 5 (iv): Hydraulic Power**

$$P_{hyd} = \frac{P \times Q}{600} = \frac{100 \times 40}{600} = 6.667 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{P_{hyd} = 6.67 \text{ kW}}$$

*(Areas – 1 mark; F_ext – 2 marks; F_ret – 1.5 marks; v & P_hyd – 2.5 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a hydraulic circuit using a 4/3 directional control valve with meter-out speed control for a double-acting cylinder. State any two advantages of meter-out control. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit Description:**

In this circuit, the flow control valve is placed on the **return (exhaust) line** of the cylinder, i.e., between the cylinder return port and the tank.

**Working:**
- **DCV in Position 1 (Extend):** Pump flow → Port A (cap end) → piston extends. Exhaust oil from Port B passes through the **meter-out flow control valve** → tank. The throttling on the return side restricts the rate at which oil leaves the cylinder, thereby controlling extension speed.
- **DCV in Position 2 (Retract):** Pump flow → Port B (rod end) → piston retracts. Exhaust oil from Port A passes through the meter-out valve → tank, controlling retraction speed.
- **DCV Centre Position (Neutral):** Pump flow is blocked; cylinder is held in position (centre-closed) or unloaded (centre-tandem).

**Two Advantages of Meter-Out Control:**
1. **Positive Load Control (Cavitation Prevention):** When the load is trying to pull the piston forward (over-running load), meter-out control provides back-pressure, preventing the load from running away and avoiding cavitation on the rod side.
2. **Better Control of Resisting Loads:** Provides smoother motion for loads that resist motion (negative loads, e.g., lowering a heavy mass), as back-pressure is maintained on the actuator.

*(Working – 4 marks; Advantages – 3 marks)*

---

**(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder is required to lift a load of 30 kN. The system pressure is 120 bar. If the piston rod diameter is 0.6 times the piston diameter, determine: **(i)** The piston (bore) diameter, **(ii)** The rod diameter, and **(iii)** The retraction force available. **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Load $F = 30 \text{ kN} = 30{,}000 \text{ N}$
- System Pressure $P = 120 \text{ bar} = 120 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $d = 0.6 D$

**Step 1 (i): Find Piston (Bore) Diameter $D$**

For lifting, oil acts on the full piston area (extension condition):

$$F = P \times A_{ext} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

$$30{,}000 = 120 \times 10^5 \times \frac{\pi}{4} \times D^2$$

$$30{,}000 = 9.4248 \times 10^6 \times D^2$$

$$D^2 = \frac{30{,}000}{9.4248 \times 10^6} = 3.183 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$D = 0.0564 \text{ m} = 56.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Standardised to nearest standard size: $D = 60 \text{ mm}$ (assumed here for force calculation).**

**Step 2 (ii): Rod Diameter $d$**

$$d = 0.6 \times D = 0.6 \times 56.4 = 33.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 (iii): Retraction Force $F_{ret}$**

Using $D = 56.4 \text{ mm} = 0.0564 \text{ m}$ and $d = 33.84 \text{ mm} = 0.03384 \text{ m}$:

$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0564^2 - 0.03384^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.003181 - 0.001145) = 1.6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{ret} = P \times A_{ret} = 120 \times 10^5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-3} = 19{,}200 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{ret} = 19.2 \text{ kN}}$$

*(D calculation – 3 marks; d calculation – 1.5 marks; F_ret – 2.5 marks)*

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (Most Common — 1 to 2 marks lost):** Forgetting to convert **bar → Pa** (multiply by 10⁵) or **L/min → m³/s** (multiply by 10⁻³/60). Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusion between $A_{ext}$ and $A_{ret}$:** During extension, use the **full piston area** ($\pi D^2/4$). During retraction, use the **annular area** ($\pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$). Mixing these leads to wrong force values.
> 3. **Skipping the Power Formula Derivation:** For 7-mark questions, examiners expect the formula $P = (P_{bar} \times Q_{LPM})/600$ **with explanation** — not just numerical substitution.
> 4. **Incorrect Decimal in $\pi$:** Use $\pi = 3.1416$ for calculations; do not write $22/7$ in SI-based hydraulic problems.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention Pascal's Law:** Every basic hydraulic numerical must reference Pascal's Law at the start of the solution. Examiners allocate 1 mark for stating the governing law.
> 6. **No Diagram for Descriptive Questions:** 5–7 mark theory questions without a circuit/ block diagram are penalised by 1–2 marks. Always include a labelled diagram.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pascal's Law**: Pressure applied to a confined incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions — *the foundational principle of all hydraulics*.
- ✅ **Force Formula**: $F = P \times A$ → *force is pressure multiplied by area*. Pressure is constant in the fluid; only area changes for force multiplication.
- ✅ **Cylinder Areas**: $A_{ext} = \pi D^2/4$ (extension), $A_{ret} = \pi(D^2 - d^2)/4$ (retraction). Retraction force is always **less than** extension force.
- ✅ **Flow Rate Formula**: $Q = A \times v$ → larger bore = slower speed for a given flow rate.
- ✅ **Hydraulic Power**: $P_{(kW)} = (P_{bar} \times Q_{LPM})/600$ → *memorise this exam-shortcut formula*.
- ✅ **Standard Conversions**: 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa, 1 L/min = 1.667 × 10⁻⁵ m³/s, 1 m = 1000 mm, 1 kN = 1000 N.
- ✅ **Directional Control Valves (DCV)**: Labelled as $X/Y$ — where $X$ = number of ports, $Y$ = number of positions. A 4/3 DCV is the most common (extend, retract, neutral).
- ✅ **Three types of speed control**: Meter-in (controls inlet), Meter-out (controls outlet), Bleed-off (diverts excess flow to tank). Meter-out is best for over-running loads.
- ✅ **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)**: Set at 10–15% above working pressure for safety margin. Always returns excess oil to tank.
- ✅ **Overall Efficiency**: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{volumetric} \times \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{hydraulic}$ — typically 75–90% for industrial systems.
- ✅ **Filtration Standard**: Suction filter = 100–150 microns; Pressure filter = 10–25 microns; Return filter = 25–40 microns.
- ✅ **Ideal Oil Temperature**: 40–60 °C. Above 80 °C → oil degrades; below 20 °C → viscosity too high.
- ✅ **KTU Lab Viva Favourites**: (a) Why is hydraulic oil used instead of water? (b) What is the function of an accumulator? (c) Differentiate meter-in vs. meter-out. (d) What is cavitation and how is it prevented?
- ✅ **Exam Strategy**: Always state **Given Data**, **To Find**, **Formula**, **Substitution with Units**, and **Final Answer with Unit** — examiners reward structured presentation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electro-Hydraulic System (EHS)** is an integrated power transmission and control system in which an **electrical signal** (low power, low energy) is used to command, modulate, and regulate a **hydraulic power stage** (high power, high force/flow) to perform precise mechanical work. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme Automation Laboratory, Module Experiment 2 typically investigates the **static and dynamic response of an electro-hydraulic actuator** — a hydraulic cylinder or motor whose spool position, and hence flow/pressure to the actuator, is governed by an electrically controlled proportional or servo valve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Electro-hydraulic systems combine the high power density of hydraulics with the flexibility and precision of electrical control. The electrical command signal (typically 0–10 V or 4–20 mA) drives an electromagnetic torque motor, which positions a flapper/spool, regulating hydraulic flow to the actuator.*

The complete signal flow is:

$$\text{Electrical Command} \rightarrow \text{Torque Motor} \rightarrow \text{Spool Valve} \rightarrow \text{Hydraulic Fluid} \rightarrow \text{Actuator (Cylinder/Motor)} \rightarrow \text{Mechanical Motion}$$

Key components encountered in the experiment are:

1. **Torque Motor / Solenoid** – Converts electrical current to a proportional mechanical force/torque.
2. **Proportional / Servo Valve** – A multi-stage hydraulic valve with closed-loop spool position feedback.
3. **Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU)** – Reservoir, pump, filter, pressure relief, and accumulator.
4. **Actuator** – Double-acting hydraulic cylinder or hydraulic motor.
5. **Feedback Transducer** – LVDT (position), pressure transducer, or flow meter for closed-loop control.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Power Steering" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of the **power steering in a modern car**. When you turn the steering wheel, you are applying a *small mechanical force*. A sensor detects this, sends an *electrical signal* to a valve, and the valve directs high-pressure hydraulic oil from the pump to assist the wheels. You (the small input) don't push the car; the **hydraulics do the heavy work**, but they listen to your *electrical* command. An electro-hydraulic system is precisely this idea, generalized and refined for industrial automation — the **electrical brain commands the hydraulic muscle**.

Another useful analogy: the **human nervous system + biceps**. The brain sends a tiny electrical pulse through nerves → muscles (hydraulic actuators) produce huge force. Electro-hydraulics mimics this bio-mechanical paradigm.

### 1.3 Core Terminology and Standard Metrics (in bold)

- **Command Signal (U)** – Input voltage/current, typically **0–10 V DC** or **4–20 mA**.
- **Spool Displacement (x_v)** – Linear movement of the valve spool from neutral, in **mm**.
- **Valve Flow Gain (K_v)** – Rate of change of load flow with spool displacement, in **m³/s per m** or **L/min per mm**.
- **Pressure Gain (K_p)** – Rate of change of load pressure with spool displacement.
- **Flow (Q)** – Hydraulic fluid flow rate, in **L/min** or **m³/s**.
- **System Pressure (P_s)** – Supply pressure, typically **70 bar (≈ 7 MPa)** in lab rigs.
- **Load Pressure (P_L)** – Pressure drop across the actuator, in **bar** or **Pa**.
- **Null / Dead Zone** – Region of command signal producing no spool motion.
- **Hysteresis** – Difference in output for rising vs. falling input, ideally **< 3 %** for servo valves.
- **Bandwidth** – Frequency up to which the system faithfully tracks a sinusoidal command, in **Hz**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU viva, you will almost certainly be asked *"Why use hydraulics when we have electric motors?"* — The answer is **power density**: a hydraulic cylinder the size of your forearm can lift a car, whereas an electric motor of equivalent size cannot. Hydraulics are **unbeatable for high-force, high-torque applications** (presses, injection moulding, aircraft flight controls, excavators).

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block Diagram Logic of the Electro-Hydraulic Loop

The experiment is designed to study the system as a **closed-loop position (or pressure) servo**. The logical flow is:

- **Step 1: Command Generation** — A function generator or DAQ card produces a step/ramp/sine waveform (electrical command $u_c$).
- **Step 2: Error Computation** — The controller (analog or PLC) computes the error $e(t) = u_c(t) - u_{fb}(t)$, where $u_{fb}$ is the LVDT/pressure feedback.
- **Step 3: Valve Actuation** — The error drives a current amplifier feeding the **torque motor** of the servo valve. The torque motor deflects the flapper, causing the spool to shift by $x_v$.
- **Step 4: Hydraulic Power Stage** — Spool displacement exposes flow paths; pump oil at pressure $P_s$ flows to the actuator cylinder. Flow rate $Q_L = K_v \cdot x_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$.
- **Step 5: Mechanical Output** — Hydraulic pressure acts on piston area $A_p$, producing force $F = P_L \cdot A_p$, which moves the load with velocity $v = Q_L / A_p$.
- **Step 6: Feedback** — The LVDT measures piston position $x_p$ and sends it back to the controller, closing the loop.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Design Choices

- **Why a torque motor and not a simple solenoid?** A simple solenoid provides only ON/OFF action. A **torque motor produces a force proportional to current**, enabling proportional control with smooth, continuous spool movement.
- **Why two-stage valves?** A single-stage proportional valve cannot generate enough force to overcome high pressure differentials at the spool. A **two-stage valve** uses a small pilot (electromechanical) stage to hydraulically position the larger main stage — this is what gives servo valves their **high dynamic response** (bandwidth 50–200 Hz).
- **Why closed loop?** Without feedback, spool forces from load pressure would push the spool to an unpredictable position. **Mechanical feedback** (a spring/lever from the main spool to the pilot) or **electrical feedback** (LVDT) linearizes the relationship between command and flow.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` instead of `|` in any LaTeX inside tables to avoid breaking markdown.**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|----------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Load Flow (orifice model) | $Q_L = K_v \, x_v \, \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | m³/s | Flow through a spool valve port |
| 2 | Linearized load flow | $Q_L = K_q \, x_v - K_c \, P_L$ | m³/s | Small-signal model around operating point |
| 3 | Flow gain | $K_q = \dfrac{\partial Q_L}{\partial x_v}\Big\vert_{P_L = \text{const}}$ | m²/s | Sensitivity of flow to spool shift |
| 4 | Pressure-flow coefficient | $K_c = \dfrac{\partial Q_L}{\partial P_L}\Big\vert_{x_v = \text{const}}$ | m³/(s·Pa) | Leakage/flow-pressure slope |
| 5 | Cylinder force balance | $A_p \, P_L = M \, \ddot{x}_p + B \, \dot{x}_p + F_L$ | N | Newton's 2nd law for piston + load |
| 6 | Continuity equation | $Q_L = A_p \, \dot{x}_p + C_{tp} \, P_L + \dfrac{V_t}{4\beta_e} \, \dot{P}_L$ | m³/s | Conservation of fluid mass |
| 7 | Spool force balance | $m_v \, \ddot{x}_v + B_v \, \dot{x}_v + K_{sp} \, x_v = K_i \, i - K_f \, P_L$ | N | Pilot-stage dynamics |
| 8 | Torque motor gain | $K_i = \dfrac{\partial T_m}{\partial i}$ | N·m/A | Electrical-to-mechanical conversion |
| 9 | Steady-state velocity | $v_{ss} = \dfrac{K_q \, x_v}{A_p}$ | m/s | Ideal no-load piston speed |
| 10 | Natural frequency (symmetric cylinder) | $\omega_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \beta_e \, A_p^{2}}{V_t \, M}}$ | rad/s | Hydraulic resonance |
| 11 | Damping ratio | $\zeta_h = \dfrac{K_{ce}}{A_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{\beta_e \, M}{V_t}} + \dfrac{B}{2}\sqrt{\dfrac{V_t}{\beta_e \, M \, A_p^{2}}}$ | — | System damping (often 0.1–0.3) |
| 12 | Open-loop transfer function (position) | $\dfrac{X_p(s)}{X_v(s)} = \dfrac{K_q / A_p}{s\left(\dfrac{s^2}{\omega_h^{2}} + \dfrac{2\zeta_h}{\omega_h}\,s + 1\right)}$ | m/m | Third-order plant with integrator |

Where:
- $A_p$ = piston area (m²), $M$ = total moving mass (kg)
- $B$ = viscous damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $V_t$ = total trapped volume (m³), $\beta_e$ = effective bulk modulus of oil (**≈ 1.4 × 10⁹ Pa**)
- $C_{tp}$ = total leakage coefficient (m³/(s·Pa))
- $F_L$ = external load force (N)
- $K_{sp}$ = spool centering spring stiffness (N/m)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Electro-hydraulic systems dominate industries where **precision + brute force** must coexist:

- **Aerospace:** Primary flight control surfaces (ailerons, rudders) of large aircraft use EHS for redundancy and power density.
- **Manufacturing:** Hydraulic press brakes, injection moulding machines, CNC clamping.
- **Heavy Earthmoving:** Excavators, bulldozers — joystick (electrical) → servo valve → hydraulic boom actuation.
- **Steel & Paper Mills:** Hydraulic roll bending, tension control.
- **Marine:** Submarine control surfaces, ship steering gear.
- **Automotive:** Active suspension, automatic transmission shift control.
