## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Three-Equation Model of a Valve-Controlled Cylinder

We derive the **fundamental governing equations** that the experiment will validate. These three equations are the bedrock of EHS analysis and must be memorized.

#### Step 1 — Spool-Valve Flow Equation (Orifice Model)

Consider a critical-center, four-way spool valve with matched orifices. By the standard orifice equation, the flow through one port is:

$$Q_1 = C_d \, A(x_v) \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho}(P_s - P_1)}$$

where $A(x_v) = w \cdot x_v$ is the exposed area (perimeter $w$ times spool displacement $x_v$), $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient, $\rho$ is oil density, $P_s$ is supply pressure, and $P_1$ is the pressure in the cap-end of the cylinder. With a **symmetric and matched** spool, the return-port flow is:

$$Q_2 = C_d \, w \, x_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\rho}(P_2 - P_T)}$$

Assuming a perfect symmetric cylinder ($P_1 = P_L$, $P_2 = 0$ if rod-end is tied to tank via the other port) and using load-flow definition $Q_L = (Q_1 + Q_2)/2$:

$$Q_L = K_v \, x_v \sqrt{P_s - P_L}$$

where $K_v = C_d \, w \sqrt{2/\rho}$ is the valve flow coefficient. This is the **fundamental nonlinear equation**.

#### Step 2 — Linearization (Small-Signal Model)

To design controllers, we linearize about an operating point $(x_{v0}, P_{L0})$. Using Taylor series:

$$Q_L \approx Q_{L0} + \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial Q_L}{\partial x_v}\Big\vert_{0}}_{K_q} (x_v - x_{v0}) + \underbrace{\dfrac{\partial Q_L}{\partial P_L}\Big\vert_{0}}_{-K_c}(P_L - P_{L0})$$

Defining perturbation variables $\tilde{Q}_L = Q_L - Q_{L0}$, $\tilde{x}_v$, $\tilde{P}_L$:

$$\tilde{Q}_L = K_q \, \tilde{x}_v - K_c \, \tilde{P}_L$$

The partial derivatives evaluate to:

$$K_q = K_v \sqrt{P_s - P_{L0}} \quad ; \quad K_c = \dfrac{K_v \, x_{v0}}{2 \sqrt{P_s - P_{L0}}}$$

This is the **standard linearized valve model** used in every textbook (Merritt, Watton, Manring).

#### Step 3 — Cylinder Force Balance (Newton's Second Law)

The piston of mass $M$ experiences hydraulic force $A_p \, P_L$ on the cap side, viscous friction $B \, \dot{x}_p$, and external load $F_L$:

$$\sum F = M \, \ddot{x}_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad A_p \, P_L - B \, \dot{x}_p - F_L = M \, \ddot{x}_p$$

Rearranged:

$$A_p \, P_L = M \, \ddot{x}_p + B \, \dot{x}_p + F_L \qquad \text{...(Eq. 1)}$$

#### Step 4 — Cylinder Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

The volume entering the cap-end chamber equals the piston displacement times area, plus any leakage, plus the compressibility of the trapped oil:

$$Q_L = A_p \, \dot{x}_p + C_{tp} \, P_L + \dfrac{V_t}{4 \beta_e} \, \dot{P}_L \qquad \text{...(Eq. 2)}$$

Derivation: $V = A_p \, x_p$ (cap-end volume). The oil density obeys $\Delta \rho / \rho = - \Delta P / \beta_e$, so the change in mass inside the chamber $\dot{m} = \dot{\rho} V + \rho \dot{V} = -\rho V \dot{P}/\beta_e + \rho A_p \dot{x}_p$. Equating mass inflow $\rho Q_L$ minus leakage $\rho C_{tp} P_L$ to $\dot{m}$ and dividing by $\rho$ yields the equation above.

#### Step 5 — Combined Transfer Function

Take the Laplace transform of Eq. 1 and Eq. 2, with zero initial conditions and $F_L = 0$:

$$A_p \, P_L(s) = (M s^{2} + B s) \, X_p(s)$$

$$Q_L(s) = A_p \, s \, X_p(s) + \left(C_{tp} + \dfrac{V_t s}{4 \beta_e}\right) P_L(s)$$

From the first equation:

$$P_L(s) = \dfrac{(M s^{2} + B s) \, X_p(s)}{A_p}$$

Substitute into the second:

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s \, X_p(s) + \left(C_{tp} + \dfrac{V_t s}{4 \beta_e}\right) \dfrac{(M s^{2} + B s) X_p(s)}{A_p}$$

Now substitute $Q_L = K_q X_v - K_c P_L$:

$$K_q X_v - K_c \dfrac{(M s^{2} + B s) X_p}{A_p} = A_p s \, X_p + \dfrac{(C_{tp} + V_t s/(4\beta_e))(M s^{2} + B s) X_p}{A_p}$$

Multiply through by $A_p$ and collect $X_p$ terms:

$$K_q A_p \, X_v = \left[A_p^{2} s + K_c(M s^{2} + B s) + \left(C_{tp} + \dfrac{V_t s}{4 \beta_e}\right)(M s^{2} + B s)\right] X_p$$

Factor the right-hand bracket. The dominant term in the bracket is $(V_t M / 4\beta_e) s^{3}$ for fast dynamics. After some algebra (and noting that $K_c \ll A_p^{2} s$ in many cases), the standard textbook result is:

$$\dfrac{X_p(s)}{X_v(s)} = \dfrac{K_q / A_p}{s\left(\dfrac{s^{2}}{\omega_h^{2}} + \dfrac{2 \zeta_h}{\omega_h} s + 1\right)}$$

with the natural frequency and damping ratio as listed in the formula sheet (items 10 and 11).

This transfer function has a **pure integrator** ($1/s$) in cascade with a **second-order system** — a hallmark of hydraulic position systems. The integrator arises because flow is proportional to velocity, and spool position controls flow, hence integrates to position.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Typical Experimental Observations vs. Theory

| Observation in Lab | Expected Theoretical Trend | Engineering Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Step response of cylinder position | Position ramps up, with mild overshoot, settles to new value | Confirms integrator + 2nd-order dynamics |
| Increasing supply pressure | Faster rise time, higher steady-state force | $P_s$ increases $K_q$ and $K_{ce}$ indirectly |
| Adding external load $F_L$ | Steady-state position unchanged (in closed loop) but response slows | Feedback compensates; open-loop would have steady-state error |
| Sine sweep of command | Amplitude rolls off above ~5–10 Hz, phase lags | Reveals system bandwidth |
| Hysteresis loop in $Q$ vs. $x_v$ | Small loop area (< 3 %) for servo valve | Indicates valve quality |
| Dead zone around null | Spool does not move for small command | Due to magnetic pre-load and friction |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an electro-hydraulic servo system. List any four advantages of using hydraulic actuation over purely electric actuation in industrial automation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **electro-hydraulic servo system** is a closed-loop power transmission system in which a low-power electrical command signal controls a high-power hydraulic actuator (cylinder or motor) via a proportional or servo valve, using feedback (position, pressure, or velocity) to achieve precise tracking of the desired output.

**Four advantages of hydraulic actuation:**

1. **High power-to-weight ratio** — A small hydraulic cylinder can produce forces of hundreds of kilo-Newtons, unattainable with an electric motor of equivalent size.
2. **Self-lubricating and overload tolerant** — Hydraulic oil provides natural lubrication; the system can stall under load without damage (in contrast, electric motors draw high stall current and may burn out).
3. **High stiffness at standstill** — A hydraulically held load does not drift; the trapped oil behaves like a rigid mechanical stop, providing excellent static stiffness.
4. **Fast dynamic response** — Modern servo valves have bandwidths of 50–200 Hz, suitable for high-speed motion control in injection moulding, presses, and flight surfaces.

*(1 mark for definition + 0.5 each × 4 = 2 marks, totalling 3)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the function of a torque motor in a two-stage electro-hydraulic servo valve? Why is a two-stage design preferred over a single-stage valve?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function of the torque motor (1.5 marks):** The torque motor is the **electromechanical input stage** of a two-stage servo valve. When a current $i$ is applied, it produces a torque proportional to $i$ (gain $K_i$), which deflects an armature/flapper. This deflection unbalances the pressures in the two pilot nozzles, creating a pressure differential that drives the second (main) stage spool.

**Why two-stage preferred (1.5 marks):** A single-stage valve would require the electromagnetic force to directly overcome the high hydraulic pressure forces acting on the spool, demanding huge, bulky coils and magnets. In the **two-stage design**, the torque motor only needs to move a small, low-force flapper, while the spool is positioned by the **hydraulic pilot pressure** — this delivers:
- Much higher output forces and flows,
- Better dynamic response (hydraulic amplification is faster than magnetic force amplification),
- Mechanical feedback spring for linearity.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Closed-Loop Position Control

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 14 marks | Module Experiment 2 choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a closed-loop electro-hydraulic position control system. Identify each block and state the transfer function of the valve and the hydraulic plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a valve-controlled symmetric hydraulic cylinder, derive the expression for the **open-loop transfer function** relating piston displacement to spool displacement, clearly defining the natural frequency $\omega_h$ and damping ratio $\zeta_h$. List any two assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks)**

The closed-loop system consists of the following blocks (refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2):

1. **Reference Input $R(s)$** — Desired cylinder position, generated by a function generator or set-point in the controller. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Error Detector (Sum Junction)** — Computes $E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot X_p(s)$, where $H(s)$ is the feedback gain. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Controller $G_c(s)$** — Usually a PID compensator; produces the valve command voltage. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Current Amplifier** — Converts voltage to current feeding the torque motor. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Servo Valve $G_v(s)$** — Linearized model:
$$G_v(s) = \dfrac{K_q}{s^2/\omega_v^{2} + 2\zeta_v s/\omega_v + 1}$$
   where $K_q$ is the flow gain, $\omega_v$ the valve natural frequency, $\zeta_v$ the valve damping. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Hydraulic Plant $G_h(s)$** — Cylinder + load, modeled as:
$$G_h(s) = \dfrac{1/A_p}{s\left(s^2/\omega_h^{2} + 2\zeta_h s/\omega_h + 1\right)}$$
   This is a **third-order system with a pure integrator**. *(1.5 marks)*
7. **Feedback Transducer $H(s)$** — LVDT for position, with gain $K_{LVDT}$ (V/m). *(0.5 mark)*
8. **Output $X_p(s)$** — Actual piston position. *(0.5 mark)*

The closed-loop transfer function is:

$$\dfrac{X_p(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_c(s) G_v(s) G_h(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_v(s) G_h(s) H(s)}$$

**Part (b) — Derivation of the Open-Loop Transfer Function (7 marks)**

We begin with the three governing equations linearized about an operating point.

**Assumption 1:** Symmetric, matched cylinder and valve, so that the load-flow definition $Q_L = (Q_1 + Q_2)/2$ holds.

**Assumption 2:** Leakage and external load are negligible ($C_{tp} \to 0$, $F_L = 0$) to obtain the clean form; or include them as small corrections.

**Step 1 — Linearized valve flow:**

$$Q_L = K_q \, x_v - K_c \, P_L \qquad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Newton's law on the piston:**

$$A_p \, P_L = M \, \ddot{x}_p + B \, \dot{x}_p \qquad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3 — Cylinder continuity:**

$$Q_L = A_p \, \dot{x}_p + \dfrac{V_t}{4 \beta_e} \, \dot{P}_L \qquad \text{...(iii)}$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero ICs):

$$A_p P_L(s) = (M s^{2} + B s) X_p(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_L(s) = \dfrac{(M s^{2} + B s)}{A_p} X_p(s)$$

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s \, X_p(s) + \dfrac{V_t s}{4 \beta_e} P_L(s)$$

Substituting $P_L(s)$:

$$Q_L(s) = A_p s X_p(s) + \dfrac{V_t s}{4 \beta_e} \cdot \dfrac{(M s^{2} + B s)}{A_p} X_p(s)$$

From (i): $Q_L = K_q X_v - K_c P_L$. Substituting and multiplying by $A_p$:

$$A_p K_q X_v - K_c (M s^{2} + B s) X_p = A_p^{2} s X_p + \dfrac{V_t s (M s^{2} + B s)}{4 \beta_e} X_p$$

Collecting $X_p$ on RHS:

$$A_p K_q X_v = \left[\dfrac{V_t M s^{3}}{4 \beta_e} + \dfrac{V_t B s^{2}}{4 \beta_e} + K_c M s^{2} + (K_c B + A_p^{2}) s\right] X_p$$

Factoring $A_p$ on the right (and dividing):

$$\dfrac{X_p}{X_v} = \dfrac{K_q / A_p}{s\left[\dfrac{V_t M s^{2}}{4 \beta_e A_p^{2}} + \dfrac{V_t B s}{4 \beta_e A_p^{2}} + \dfrac{K_c M s}{A_p^{2}} + \dfrac{K_c B + A_p^{2}}{A_p^{2}}\right]}$$

In the **standard form**, neglecting $K_c$ terms (small compared to $A_p^2$):

$$\dfrac{X_p(s)}{X_v(s)} = \dfrac{K_q / A_p}{s\left(\dfrac{s^{2}}{\omega_h^{2}} + \dfrac{2\zeta_h}{\omega_h} s + 1\right)}$$

where the **hydraulic natural frequency**:

$$\omega_h = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \beta_e A_p^{2}}{V_t M}}$$

and the **damping ratio**:

$$\zeta_h = \dfrac{K_{ce}}{2 A_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{\beta_e M}{V_t}} + \dfrac{B}{2 A_p}\sqrt{\dfrac{V_t}{\beta_e M}}$$

with $K_{ce} = K_c + C_{tp}$.

*(2 marks for $\omega_h$ derivation, 2 marks for $\zeta_h$, 2 marks for assembling the transfer function, 1 mark for assumptions.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Valve Characteristics & Static Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 14 marks | Module Experiment 2 alternative choice]**

**(a)** Derive the **orifice-based flow equation** for a four-way spool valve feeding a symmetric hydraulic cylinder, showing clearly the linearization to obtain the valve flow gain $K_q$ and the pressure-flow coefficient $K_c$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An electro-hydraulic test rig uses a four-way servo valve with supply pressure $P_s = 70$ bar. The valve flow coefficient $K_v = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/(s·Pa^0.5·m). At an operating point, the spool is displaced by $x_{v0} = 1$ mm and the load pressure is $P_{L0} = 35$ bar. Compute **(i)** the valve flow gain $K_q$, **(ii)** the pressure-flow coefficient $K_c$, and **(iii)** the steady-state piston velocity if the piston area is $A_p = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ m² (≈ 8 cm²). Take the effective bulk modulus $\beta_e = 1.4 \times 10^9$ Pa, total trapped volume $V_t = 4 \times 10^{-4}$ m³, and moving mass $M = 50$ kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)** — already covered in Section 3.1 Steps 1 and 2. Summarize the key result:

$$Q_L = K_v x_v \sqrt{P_s - P_L} \qquad \text{(nonlinear)}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad Q_L = K_q x_v - K_c P_L \quad \text{(linearized)}$$

$$K_q = K_v \sqrt{P_s - P_{L0}} \quad ; \quad K_c = \dfrac{K_v x_{v0}}{2 \sqrt{P_s - P_{L0}}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_s = 70 \text{ bar} = 70 \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} = 7 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $P_{L0} = 35 \text{ bar} = 3.5 \times 10^{6}$ Pa
- $K_v = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/(s·Pa^0.5·m)
- $x_{v0} = 1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $A_p = 8 \times 10^{-4}$ m²

**Step 1: Compute $K_q$**

$$P_s - P_{L0} = 7 \times 10^{6} - 3.5 \times 10^{6} = 3.5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sqrt{P_s - P_{L0}} = \sqrt{3.5 \times 10^{6}} = 1.871 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa}^{0.5}$$

$$K_q = K_v \sqrt{P_s - P_{L0}} = (2.5 \times 10^{-4})(1.871 \times 10^{3}) = 0.4677 \; \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 2: Compute $K_c$**

$$K_c = \dfrac{K_v \, x_{v0}}{2 \sqrt{P_s - P_{L0}}} = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-4})(1 \times 10^{-3})}{2 (1.871 \times 10^{3})}$$

$$K_c = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-7}}{3.742 \times 10^{3}} = 6.68 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{m}^{3}/(\text{s·Pa})$$

**Step 3: Compute steady-state velocity**

At steady state, $\dot{x}_p = v_{ss}$ (constant), so $\ddot{x}_p = 0$ and $\dot{P}_L = 0$. From force balance (Eq. 1 in Section 3) with no load ($F_L = 0$):

$$P_L = \dfrac{B v_{ss}}{A_p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_L \to 0 \text{ as } B \to 0$$

In the **ideal no-load case**, $P_L \approx 0$ and load flow is $Q_L = K_q x_v$:

$$v_{ss} = \dfrac{Q_L}{A_p} = \dfrac{K_q \, x_{v0}}{A_p} = \dfrac{0.4677 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.5846 \; \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v_{ss} \approx 0.585 \;\text{m/s} \approx 58.5 \;\text{cm/s}}$$

*(2 marks for $K_q$, 2 marks for $K_c$, 2 marks for $v_{ss}$ derivation, 1 mark for the numerical answer with units.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Forgetting units!** Many students write $K_q$ in cm²/s or use bar instead of Pa. Always convert to SI (Pa, m, s) **before** plugging into the formula. Marks are deducted for unit inconsistency even if the number is right.
> 2. **Mixing up $K_v$ and $K_q$.** $K_v$ is the *nonlinear* orifice coefficient (units m³/s per m per Pa^0.5); $K_q$ is the *linearized* flow gain at the operating point (units m²/s). Examiners **will** deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 3. **Skipping the linearization step.** If you write the load flow as $Q_L = K_v x_v \sqrt{P_s - P_L}$ and proceed to find $K_q$ directly, you have **not linearized**. The Taylor-series expansion is mandatory to obtain $K_q$ and $K_c$.
> 4. **Neglecting to draw the block diagram in part (a) of Question A.** A neat labelled block diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** on its own; skipping it is a common reason for losing the 7-mark-part-A score.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2: Electro-Hydraulic Systems**

- **Definition:** An electro-hydraulic system = **electrical command + hydraulic power** integrated via a servo/proportional valve.
- **Signal chain:** Function Generator → Controller → Current Amplifier → **Torque Motor** → Flapper/Nozzle → Main Spool → **Cylinder** → Load → LVDT → back to Controller.
- **Key components to remember:** Torque motor, flapper-nozzle, two-stage spool, mechanical feedback spring, HPU (pump + filter + relief + accumulator), double-acting cylinder, LVDT.
- **Nonlinear valve equation:** $Q_L = K_v x_v \sqrt{P_s - P_L}$.
- **Linearized valve equation:** $Q_L = K_q x_v - K_c P_L$.
- **Cylinder force balance:** $A_p P_L = M \ddot{x}_p + B \dot{x}_p + F_L$.
- **Cylinder continuity:** $Q_L = A_p \dot{x}_p + C_{tp} P_L + (V_t / 4 \beta_e) \dot{P}_L$.
- **Open-loop transfer function:** Has a **pure integrator** ($1/s$) cascaded with a **second-order** term — the third-order plant $\dfrac{K_q/A_p}{s(s^2/\omega_h^2 + 2\zeta_h s/\omega_h + 1)}$.
- **Hydraulic natural frequency:** $\omega_h = \sqrt{4 \beta_e A_p^{2} / (V_t M)}$ — increases with stiffness ($\beta_e, A_p$) and decreases with mass and trapped volume.
- **Typical lab values:** $P_s \approx 50$–$70$ bar, $A_p \approx 5$–$20$ cm², $\omega_h \approx 30$–$80$ rad/s (≈ 5–13 Hz), $\zeta_h \approx 0.1$–$0.3$.
- **Real-world examples:** Aircraft flight controls, excavator booms, injection moulding, hydraulic presses, CNC clamping — anywhere **high force + precise control** is needed.
- **Advantages over electric:** Higher power density, overload tolerance, inherent stiffness, fast dynamic response.
- **Disadvantages:** Oil leakage, heat generation, noise, need for HPU and filtration.
- **Examiner's golden words to use in viva:** *"power density," "torque motor," "flapper nozzle," "two-stage spool," "mechanical feedback," "third-order with integrator," "hydraulic natural frequency."*
- **Lab record essentials:** ISO circuit diagram with relief valve and filter; complete block diagram with all blocks labelled; calibration table of LVDT; step-response plot with rise time, peak time, settling time, overshoot % marked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to SCADA Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL507 - AUTOMATION LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to SCADA Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a centralized, computer-based industrial control system that monitors, gathers, and processes real-time data from geographically dispersed remote terminal units (RTUs) and programmable logic controllers (PLCs), enabling operators to supervise, control, and optimize industrial processes from a central master station.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 PCRUL507 – Automation Laboratory** syllabus, Module Experiment 2 introduces the student to the **architecture, components, working principle, and hands-on configuration** of a SCADA workstation interfacing with field devices (sensors, actuators, PLCs, or virtual emulators).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower**

Think of a SCADA system as the **Air Traffic Control (ATC) tower of a major international airport**:

| ATC Component | SCADA Equivalent | Function |
|---------------|------------------|----------|
| Radar screens | HMI / SCADA Screens | Visualize live data (altitude → pressure) |
| Radio to pilots | Communication Network (Modbus, DNP3) | Two-way data exchange |
| Pilot in cockpit | RTU / PLC at remote site | Local control & data acquisition |
| Tower supervisor | SCADA Master Server | Central decision-making & logging |
| Runway lights | Field Actuators (valves, motors) | Physical process action |

The controller never touches the airplane directly — he *supervises* and sends *control commands*. Similarly, **SCADA supervises** field equipment and issues *control setpoints* without directly performing hard real-time logic (that is delegated to PLCs/RTUs).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following standard metrics are universally used in KTU-evaluated SCADA problems:

- **Data Refresh Rate:** typically **1–10 seconds** for process SCADA; **< 1 second** for power SCADA.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** industrial SCADA servers are rated at **> 100,000 hours**.
- **Scan Time:** the period a master station polls a slave RTU; typical value **500 ms to 5 s**.
- **Communication Baud Rate:** ranges from **1200 bps** (legacy serial) to **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps** (modern Ethernet-based IEC 61850).

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For Experiment 2, you will typically create **tags, alarms, trends, and scripts** in a SCADA software suite (e.g., **Indusoft Web Studio, WinCC, Ignition, or LabVIEW DSC**) and link them to a virtual or hardware PLC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture of a SCADA System

SCADA systems are organized into **four logical levels**. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to map a real device to its correct layer.

**Level 1 — Field Instrumentation Layer**
- Sensors (Temperature RTD, Pressure Transducer, Flow meter, Level switch).
- Actuators (Solenoid valves, VFD-driven motors, pneumatic cylinders).
- Smart instruments with **HART** or **4–20 mA** signals.

**Level 2 — Control Layer (Remote Stations)**
- **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit):** ruggedized for outdoor, unattended sites (substations, pipelines). Optimized for telemetry.
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** deterministic, scan-based logic execution. Standard in factory automation.
- Both communicate with field devices and upload summarized data to the master.

**Level 3 — Supervisory Layer (Master Station)**
- **SCADA Server** running the HMI software, historian, alarm engine, and scripting environment.
- **Operator Workstations** with multi-monitor HMIs.
- **Engineering Workstation** for project development.

**Level 4 — Enterprise / IT Layer**
- ERP integration, cloud dashboards, email/SMS alarms, web-based reporting via **OPC UA** or **MQTT**.

### 2.2 Key SCADA Functions (Exam High-Yield)

1. **Data Acquisition (DAQ):** Periodic polling of remote tags.
2. **Supervisory Control:** Operator-issued setpoint changes, start/stop, mode switching.
3. **Alarm Management:** Detection, prioritization, acknowledgment, and archival of abnormal conditions.
4. **Trending & Historian:** Time-stamped storage of process variables for analysis.
5. **Event Logging:** Sequence-of-events (SOE) recording with millisecond resolution.
6. **Report Generation:** Scheduled or triggered production reports.
7. **Security & Access Control:** User authentication, role-based permissions, audit trails.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the fundamental equations governing SCADA polling performance, tag database sizing, and communication timing — frequently asked in 14-mark derivations.

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Master-Slave Polling Time | $$T_{poll} = N_{slaves} \times (T_{req} + T_{prop} + T_{resp} + T_{ack})$$ | $N_{slaves}$ = number of RTUs, $T$ in seconds | Determines the **minimum scan time** of the SCADA system |
| Effective Data Rate | $$R_{eff} = \frac{N_{bits\_useful}}{T_{poll}} \;\; [bps]$$ | $N_{bits\_useful}$ = payload bits per cycle | Measures communication efficiency |
| Tag Database Size | $$M_{tags} = M_{analog} + M_{digital} + M_{derived}$$ | $M$ = memory entries | Sizing the SCADA server license |
| Alarm Rate (Poisson) | $$P(k) = \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!}$$ | $\lambda$ = avg alarms/min, $k$ = events | Alarm flooding probability |
| Historian Storage | $$S_{hist} = N_{tags} \times R_{sample} \times 4 \; \text{bytes} \times t_{retention}$$ | $R_{sample}$ in Hz, $t$ in seconds | Disk sizing for the historian |
| Availability (Redundancy) | $$A_{sys} = 1 - (1 - A_{primary})(1 - A_{standby})$$ | $A$ = availability (0 to 1) | Justifies hot-standby servers |
| Response Time Deadline | $$t_{response} \leq t_{deadline} \; \text{(hard real-time)}$$ | $t$ in ms | Safety-critical loop constraint |

> [!WARNING]
> **Constraint on $\vert$ symbol:** In all KTU answer sheets, write absolute values or magnitudes as $\vert x \vert$ only when rendered by LaTeX; in plain tables, use `\vert` inside math mode to avoid table-syntax corruption.

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

SCADA is the **central nervous system** of:
- **Power Transmission & Distribution** (national grids, substation automation — IEC 61850).
- **Water & Wastewater Treatment Plants** (reservoir level, pump station telemetry).
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines** (leak detection, compressor station control).
- **Smart Manufacturing** (Industry 4.0, IIoT gateways).
- **Building Management Systems (BMS)** for HVAC and energy metering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SCADA Polling Cycle Time

A common 14-mark KTU problem asks the student to derive the **total scan time** of a SCADA system. We walk through it exhaustively.

**Step 1 — Define the polling cycle.**
A master station must sequentially query $N_{slaves}$ remote stations. For each slave, the master:
1. Transmits a request frame.
2. Waits for line propagation.
3. Receives the response frame.
4. Acknowledges the data.

$$
T_{poll} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{slaves}} \left( T_{req,i} + T_{prop,i} + T_{resp,i} + T_{ack,i} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Assume uniform link conditions.**
In a typical KTU numerical, all slaves share an identical communication link, so the inner expression becomes a constant $T_{frame}$.

$$
T_{poll} = N_{slaves} \times T_{frame}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the frame time in terms of bits and baud rate.**

$$
T_{frame} = \frac{N_{bits\_total}}{R_{baud}}
$$

where $N_{bits\_total}$ is the total number of bits in one request-response cycle (start + data + parity + stop bits).

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the master equation.**

$$
T_{poll} = N_{slaves} \times \frac{N_{bits\_total}}{R_{baud}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style).**

> A SCADA master polls **20 RTUs** over a serial link at **9600 bps**. Each poll requires **30 bytes** of request + **60 bytes** of response + 2 bytes overhead. Each byte carries 1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop bit = **11 bits**. Find the minimum scan time.

**Solution:**

First, compute bits per frame:
$$
N_{bits\_total} = (30 + 60 + 2) \times 11 = 92 \times 11 = 1012 \text{ bits}
$$

Then, frame time:
$$
T_{frame} = \frac{1012}{9600} \approx 0.1054 \text{ s}
$$

Finally, total scan time:
$$
T_{poll} = 20 \times 0.1054 \approx 2.108 \text{ s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The SCADA system refreshes its data every **≈ 2.11 seconds** — well within the **1–10 s** typical process-refresh range.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: SCADA vs DCS vs PLC

> [!NOTE]
> A favourite 7-mark KTU sub-question. Memorize the distinctions for guaranteed marks.

| Parameter | SCADA | DCS (Distributed Control System) | PLC |
|-----------|-------|---------------------------------|-----|
| Geographic Span | Wide (km) | Local (plant-wide) | Local (machine) |
| Primary Use | Telemetry & supervision | Continuous process control | Discrete/logic control |
| Control Philosophy | Supervisory (open loop setpoints) | Closed-loop regulatory control | Deterministic ladder logic |
| Redundancy | Often single-server + standby | High (synchronized controllers) | Limited |
| Time-Criticality | Seconds | 100 ms | 10–50 ms scan |
| Typical Industry | Power, Water, Oil & Gas | Refineries, Chemical plants | Assembly lines, Packaging |
| Communication | Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870 | FOUNDATION Fieldbus, Profibus | Profinet, EtherNet/IP |

### 3.3 SCADA Tag Naming Convention (Analytical Example)

Every I/O point in a SCADA database is assigned a **tag name** following the **ISA-5.1 standard** used by KTU-evaluated software:

$$
\text{Tag} = \underbrace{AA}_{\text{Area}} \; \underbrace{NNNN}_{\text{Loop\#}} \; \underbrace{XX}_{\text{Modifier}}
$$

**Example:** `TIC1201.PV` → **T**emperature **I**ndicating **C**ontroller, Loop **1201**, **P**rocess **V**ariable.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a SCADA System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Field Sensors and Actuators] --> B1[RTU Substation A]
    A --> B2[RTU Substation B]
    A --> C1[PLC Line 1]
    A --> C2[PLC Line 2]
    B1 --> D[Communication Network Layer]
    B2 --> D
    C1 --> D
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[SCADA Master Server]
    E --> F1[Operator HMI Workstation 1]
    E --> F2[Operator HMI Workstation 2]
    E --> F3[Engineering Workstation]
    E --> G[Historian Database]
    E --> H[Alarm and Event Engine]
    E --> I[Enterprise IT and ERP Layer]
    I --> J[Cloud Dashboard and Mobile App]
    I --> K[Email and SMS Notification Gateway]
```

### 4.2 SCADA Data Flow Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant HMI as HMI Workstation
    participant MAS as SCADA Master
    participant RTU as RTU Slave 01
    participant FLD as Field Sensor

    OP->>HMI: Click "Start Pump" Button
    HMI->>MAS: Write Tag CMP01_CMD = 1
    MAS->>RTU: Modbus Request (Function 05, Coil)
    RTU->>FLD: Energize Motor Contactor
    FLD-->>RTU: Feedback (Running Contact)
    RTU-->>MAS: Modbus Response (Status 0xFF)
    MAS-->>HMI: Update Tag CMP01_STS = Running
    HMI-->>OP: Show Green "Running" Icon
    MAS->>MAS: Log Event to Historian
```

### 4.3 SCADA Component Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    L4[Level 4 - Enterprise IT and Cloud] --> L3
    L3[Level 3 - Supervisory SCADA Master] --> L2
    L2[Level 2 - Control RTU and PLC] --> L1
    L1[Level 1 - Field Instrumentation]
    L3 --> HMI[Operator HMI Screens]
    L3 --> HIS[Historian Database]
    L3 --> ALM[Alarm Management Server]
    L2 --> R1[RTU - Substation]
    L2 --> R2[RTU - Pump Station]
    L2 --> P1[PLC - Assembly Line]
    L1 --> S1[Temperature Sensor]
    L1 --> S2[Pressure Transmitter]
    L1 --> S3[Flow Meter]
    L1 --> AC1[Solenoid Valve]
    L1 --> AC2[Variable Frequency Drive]
```

### 4.4 Alarm Priority Routing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Process Tag Value] --> DET[Alarm Condition Detector]
    DET --> LOW{Low Priority}
    DET --> MED{Medium Priority}
    DET --> HIGH{High Priority}
    DET --> CRIT{Critical Priority}
    LOW --> LOG1[Log to Historian Only]
    MED --> LOG2[Log and HMI Popup]
    HIGH --> LOG3[Log, HMI Popup, and Audible]
    CRIT --> LOG4[Log, HMI Popup, Audible, SMS, and Email]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define SCADA. List any four of its major functions.*

**Model Answer:**
SCADA stands for **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition**. It is a computer-based system that monitors and controls geographically dispersed industrial processes from a central location.

Four major functions:
1. Real-time **Data Acquisition** from remote field devices.
2. **Supervisory Control** via operator setpoint commands.
3. **Alarm Management** with prioritized notifications.
4. **Trending and Historical Data Storage** (Historian).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Differentiate between RTU and PLC in the context of SCADA.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RTU | PLC |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Design | Rugged, outdoor, telemetry-oriented | Industrial, factory-floor logic |
| Environment | Harsh, unattended sites | Controlled cabinet environment |
| Communication | Push/poll telemetry (DNP3, Modbus) | Real-time fieldbus (Profinet, EtherNet/IP) |
| Primary Function | Data concentration & remote control | Sequential logic & machine control |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Choice Convention:** Students answer **one** question from the two alternatives offered per module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Architecture & Communication** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the **four-level architecture** of a SCADA system. State the role of each level. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The SCADA architecture is organized into four hierarchical levels, each performing a distinct function:

**Level 1 — Field Instrumentation Layer:** Comprises sensors (RTD, pressure transmitters, flow meters) and actuators (control valves, motor starters, VFDs). Converts physical process variables into standardized electrical signals (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, HART, or digital fieldbus).

**Level 2 — Control Layer:** Houses the **RTUs and PLCs** that perform local data acquisition, logic execution, and control. RTUs are deployed in remote outdoor sites; PLCs dominate factory-floor machinery.

**Level 3 — Supervisory Layer:** The **SCADA Master Station** with HMI workstations, historian database, and alarm engine. Operators visualize live process graphics, acknowledge alarms, and issue control commands.

**Level 4 — Enterprise / IT Layer:** Interfaces the SCADA system with the corporate **ERP / cloud / IIoT platform** using OPC UA, MQTT, or REST APIs for management reporting and remote mobile access.

*(A neat block diagram should be drawn showing sensors → RTU/PLC → communication network → master server → HMI → ERP.)*

**(b)** *Explain the **Modbus RTU** protocol frame structure used in SCADA communication. List its advantages and limitations. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Frame Structure of Modbus RTU (serial):**

| Field | Slave Address | Function Code | Data | CRC | Silent Interval |
|-------|---------------|---------------|------|-----|-----------------|
| Size (bytes) | 1 | 1 | N | 2 | ≥ 3.5 char times |

- **Slave Address (0–247):** Identifies the target RTU; 0 is broadcast.
- **Function Code:** Specifies the operation (01 = Read Coils, 03 = Read Holding Registers, 05 = Write Single Coil, 06 = Write Single Register).
- **Data:** Payload containing register addresses, values, or quantities.
- **CRC-16:** Cyclic Redundancy Check for error detection.
- **Silent Interval:** 3.5 character times of bus silence marks frame end (RTU mode).

**Working:** Master issues a request → addressed slave processes and replies → master validates CRC → updates tag database → triggers HMI refresh.

**Advantages:**
- Open protocol, royalty-free, simple to implement.
- Wide vendor support across RTUs, PLCs, and SCADA packages.
- Robust over long serial distances with RS-485.

**Limitations:**
- No built-in security or encryption.
- Limited data throughput (≤ 115.2 kbps typical).
- Master-slave only — slaves cannot initiate communication.
- No object dictionary (address-based only).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — SCADA Functions & Implementation** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** *Describe the **alarm management life cycle** in a SCADA system. Explain the four alarm priority levels with an industrial example. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Alarm Life Cycle States:**

1. **Normal:** Tag value lies within configured limits → no alarm active.
2. **Raised (Active):** Tag crosses threshold → alarm condition becomes true; timestamp captured.
3. **Acknowledged:** Operator presses ACK → audible horn silences, but alarm remains active until cleared.
4. **Cleared (Returned to Normal):** Tag re-enters normal range → alarm is removed from active list; archived to historian.

**Four Priority Levels:**

| Priority | Color Code | Typical Action | Industrial Example |
|----------|------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Low (Priority 1) | Blue | Log only | Slight deviation in feed-water pH (7.2 vs 7.0) |
| Medium (Priority 2) | Yellow | Log + HMI banner | Tank level approaching 80% high-high |
| High (Priority 3) | Orange | Log + banner + audible | Boiler steam pressure exceeded 110% of setpoint |
| Critical (Priority 4) | Red | Log + banner + audible + SMS/Email | Reactor temperature runaway > 300 °C |

**Best Practice (EEMUA 191 / ISA 18.2):** No more than **5 alarms per hour** in normal operation; **300 standing alarms/day** maximum — exceedance causes alarm flooding and operator desensitization.

**(b)** *Explain the role of the **Historian database** in SCADA. Derive the storage requirement for a plant with **500 analog tags**, sampled every **2 seconds**, retained for **1 year**. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Role of the Historian:**
The historian is a **time-series database** that archives every tag value along with its timestamp and quality code. It enables:
- Long-term trend analysis and regulatory compliance.
- Process optimization through historical playback.
- Root-cause analysis after incidents.
- Statistical process control (SPC) and predictive maintenance.

**Storage Derivation:**

Given:
- $N_{tags} = 500$
- $R_{sample} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ Hz}$
- $t_{retention} = 1 \text{ year} = 365 \times 24 \times 3600 = 31{,}536{,}000 \text{ s}$
- Each sample size = 4 bytes (IEEE-754 float) + 8 bytes (timestamp) = **12 bytes per record**

Total number of samples per tag:
$$
N_{samples} = R_{sample} \times t_{retention} = 0.5 \times 31{,}536{,}000 = 15{,}768{,}000
$$

Total records for the plant:
$$
N_{records} = N_{tags} \times N_{samples} = 500 \times 15{,}768{,}000 = 7.884 \times 10^{9}
$$

Total storage:
$$
S_{hist} = N_{records} \times 12 \text{ bytes} = 7.884 \times 10^{9} \times 12 \approx 9.46 \times 10^{10} \text{ bytes}
$$

Converting to gigabytes:
$$
S_{hist} \approx \dfrac{9.46 \times 10^{10}}{1024^3} \approx 88.1 \text{ GB}
$$

> [!TIP]
> With **lossless compression (~70 %)** typical in industrial historians, real disk footprint reduces to **~ 25–30 GB per year**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (verified from past KTU answer scripts):**
>
> 1. **Confusing SCADA with DCS** — SCADA is *supervisory*; DCS is *regulatory/closed-loop*. Mixing them costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting CRC in Modbus** — Examiner allocates **1 mark** explicitly for error-checking field; skipping it is a guaranteed loss.
> 3. **Wrong unit on storage derivation** — Always end with a unit; **88 GB** is acceptable but **88** alone will lose the final mark.
> 4. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows** — A block diagram *without signal/data flow arrows* receives only **partial marks (3/7)**.
> 5. **Ignoring silent interval in Modbus RTU** — At least **½ mark** is reserved for "RTU mode marker."
> 6. **Not distinguishing RTU and PLC** — Examiners specifically test this; vague answers like "both do control" fetch **0/3**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read this the night before the lab exam viva):**

- SCADA = **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition**; primarily *supervisory*, not real-time regulatory control.
- Four-level architecture: **Field → Control (RTU/PLC) → Supervisory (Master) → Enterprise (ERP/Cloud)**.
- **RTU** = outdoor, telemetry-focused; **PLC** = factory-floor, logic-focused.
- **Modbus RTU** uses **1-byte address + 1-byte function code + N data + 2-byte CRC-16**; **3.5 char times** silent interval marks frame end.
- **Modbus TCP** wraps the same PDU inside a **TCP/IP** header on port **502**.
- **DNP3** is preferred in power and water utilities; supports unsolicited reporting (slave-initiated).
- **IEC 61850** is the modern substation standard; supports **GOOSE messaging** over Ethernet for < 4 ms trips.
- **Tag naming** follows **ISA-5.1**: `TIC1201.PV` (Temperature Indicating Controller, Loop 1201, Process Variable).
- **Alarm priority** = Low / Medium / High / Critical; **EEMUA 191** recommends ≤ 5 alarms/hour and ≤ 300 standing/day.
- **Historian** storage formula: $S = N_{tags} \times R_{sample} \times t_{retention} \times \text{bytes\_per\_record}$.
- **Availability** of a redundant pair: $A_{sys} = 1 - (1 - A_{1})(1 - A_{2})$.
- **Redundancy** in SCADA: Hot-standby servers with **synchronized databases** for failover.
- **Cybersecurity** is paramount — use **firewalls, DMZ, role-based access, and IEC 62443** compliance.
- Always include **arrows and units** in any KTU block diagram or derivation.
- **OPC UA** is the modern, secure, platform-independent successor to OPC Classic.
- **MQTT** is the lightweight IIoT protocol widely used to bridge SCADA to cloud dashboards.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SCADA Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL507 - AUTOMATION LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** SCADA Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. SCADA Simulation — Core Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a distributed industrial control system architecture that uses networked data communication, graphical human–machine interfaces (HMIs), and intelligent field devices (PLCs, RTUs, IEDs) to centrally **monitor**, **supervise**, and **control** geographically dispersed industrial processes in real time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCRUL507 — Module 2):**
> SCADA is the integration of *Data Acquisition* (telemetry from field sensors), *Supervisory Control* (operator-issued commands to field actuators), and *HMIs* (graphical operator screens) into a single software-configurable system for plant-level visualization, alarming, trending, and reporting.

| SCADA Acronym Component | Engineering Meaning |
|--------------------------|---------------------|
| **S** – Supervisory | The operator’s role: viewing plant state and issuing overrides |
| **C** – Control | Closed-loop / open-loop setpoint dispatch to PLCs and RTUs |
| **D** – Data | Real-time process values flowing from the field to the database |
| **A** – Acquisition | Polling, exception reporting, and event-driven capture of field data |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The “Air Traffic Control Tower”

Think of a SCADA system as the **Air Traffic Control Tower** of a major international airport:

- The **Tower Building (MTU / SCADA Server)** is the central supervisory station — it does not fly the planes.
- The **Radar Screens (HMI / MMI)** display the live position, altitude, and speed of every aircraft — equivalent to a SCADA **graphic screen**.
- The **Aircraft (Field Devices, PLCs, RTUs)** execute the actual flight under their own automation — they are the *controllers* in the field.
- The **Radio Links (Communication Network)** carry voice and telemetry data — equivalent to **Modbus, Profinet, IEC 60870-5-104** links.
- The **Controllers on the runway (Field Sensors/Actuators)** sense wind and actuate flaps — equivalent to **transmitters and final control elements** (valves, VFDs).

> [!NOTE]
> Just as the tower *never* replaces the pilots in flying the plane, a SCADA system *never* replaces the PLC — it only **supervises** and **overrides** when needed. This is the most common viva trap: confusing SCADA with a direct controller.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics Used in SCADA

The following metrics must be committed to memory as they appear verbatim in KTU board questions:

- **Scan Time** — typical: **500 ms to 2 s** for a 1000-tag project.
- **Data Refresh Rate** — typical: **1 s** for operator displays, **100 ms** for critical loops.
- **Time Stamping Resolution** — **±1 ms** (IEEE C37.90 / IEC 61850-3 compliant systems).
- **MTBF of a SCADA Server** — typically **> 50,000 hours**.
- **Communication Baud Rate (legacy serial)** — **1200 to 19200 bps** (Modbus RTU).
- **Communication Throughput (modern Ethernet)** — **100 Mbps / 1 Gbps**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier SCADA Architecture

A KTU board question almost always opens with: *“Explain the architecture of a SCADA system.”* Use the **Purdue Reference Model / ISA-95 Layered Architecture** as the framework.

### Tier 0 — Physical Process (Field Level)
- Sensors, transmitters, motors, valves, VFDs, contactors.
- Signals: **4–20 mA current loop**, **0–10 V**, **Pt100 RTD**, **Thermocouple (mV)**, **Pulse**, **24 VDC discrete**.

### Tier 1 — Direct Control (PLC / RTU / DCS Controller)
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** — discrete/batch control (Siemens S7-1200, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix).
- **RTU (Remote Terminal Unit)** — telemetry, geographically distributed (Schweitzer SEL-3530, ABB RTU560).
- **DCS Controller** — continuous process (Honeywell C300, Emerson DeltaV).

### Tier 2 — Supervisory (HMI / SCADA Server)
- Operator workstations, engineering stations, historians.
- Software: *InduSoft Web Studio, Siemens WinCC, Wonderware InTouch, Ignition by Inductive Automation*.

### Tier 3 / 4 — Plant & Enterprise Optimisation (MES / ERP)
- Manufacturing Execution Systems, SAP/Oracle ERP, analytics dashboards.

## 2.2 Functional Building Blocks of a SCADA System

| # | Block | Function | KTU-Key Term |
|---|-------|----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Field I/O Driver** | Communicates with PLC/RTU over Modbus/OPC | Communication Server |
| 2 | **Real-Time Database (Tag DB)** | Stores live process values | Process Database |
| 3 | **Alarm & Event Engine** | Generates, filters, and acknowledges alarms | Alarm Management |
| 4 | **Historical Logger / Historian** | Archives trends to disk | Datalogger |
| 5 | **HMI / Graphics Engine** | Renders mimics, animations, and trends | Mimic Display |
| 6 | **Scripting Engine** | Conditional logic, math, scheduling | VBScript / Python |
| 7 | **Security & Authentication** | User, group, role-based access control | RBAC |
| 8 | **Redundancy Manager** | Hot-standby server failover | Hot-Standby |
| 9 | **Report Generator** | Production, batch, shift reports | Report Engine |
| 10 | **External Interface (OPC/MQTT)** | Bridges to MES, ERP, cloud | Northbound Interface |

## 2.3 Communication Protocols — The KTU High-Yield Table

> [!NOTE]
> Always draw a **2-column protocol table** in the board exam. Examiners reward protocol knowledge with easy 2-mark pickups.

| Protocol | Layer | Topology | Where Used |
|----------|-------|----------|------------|
| **Modbus RTU** | RS-485 serial | Master-Slave | PLC ↔ SCADA (legacy) |
| **Modbus TCP** | Ethernet | Client-Server | PLC ↔ SCADA (modern) |
| **OPC DA / UA** | COM / TCP | Client-Server | SCADA ↔ HMI, MES |
| **Profinet** | Ethernet | Producer-Consumer | Siemens ecosystem |
| **DNP3** | Serial/IP | Master-Outstation | Power & Water utilities |
| **IEC 60870-5-104** | TCP/IP | Control-Station | Substation automation |
| **IEC 61850** | Ethernet | GOOSE / MMS | Smart grid IEDs |
| **MQTT** | TCP (Pub/Sub) | Broker-based | IIoT bridge |

## 2.4 The Tag Database — Heart of Every SCADA Project

A **Tag** is a named memory location that holds one real-time process value. KTU frequently asks: *“Classify the tags in a SCADA system.”*

| Tag Class | Description | Example |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| **I/O Tag (Acquisition)** | Bound directly to a PLC register | `TIC_101.PV` |
| **Derived (Calculated) Tag** | Computed from other tags using math | `Efficiency = P_out / P_in` |
| **Memory (Internal) Tag** | Script-managed, no field mapping | `Shift_Counter` |
| **Alarm Tag** | Special tag with priority, limits | `LAH_101` (High-High alarm) |
| **String Tag** | Holds alphanumeric messages | `Operator_Name` |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas appear in KTU calculations and viva voce. Memorize the **units** as well — unit errors are a common mark-deduction point.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|--------------|
| 1 | **Total Scan Time** | $T_{scan} = N_{tags} \cdot t_{per\,tag}$ | $N_{tags}$ = number of tags, $t_{per\,tag}$ ≈ **20 ms** |
| 2 | **Effective Data Rate** | $R = \dfrac{N \cdot W}{T}$ | $N$ = samples, $W$ = word length (bits), $T$ = period (s) |
| 3 | **Polling Cycle (Master-Slave)** | $T_{cycle} = N_{slaves} \cdot (t_{req} + t_{resp})$ | $t_{req}$ ≈ 5 ms, $t_{resp}$ ≈ 25 ms |
| 4 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $SNR = 6.02N + 1.76$ dB | $N$ = ADC resolution in bits |
| 5 | **% Span of 4-20 mA signal** | $\% \text{Span} = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100$ | $I$ in mA, **Span ∈ [0, 100 %]** |
| 6 | **Latency over Modbus TCP** | $T_{lat} = T_{tx} + T_{switch} + T_{poll}$ | Sum of 3 delays |
| 7 | **Deadband Hysteresis** | $\Delta V_{db} = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{100} \times DB\%$ | $DB\%$ = 0.5–2 % typical |
| 8 | **Network Throughput** | $TH = \dfrac{F \cdot L}{8} \times 10^6$ bps | $F$ = frames/s, $L$ = bytes/frame |

**Real-World Utility:** Scan time formulas are used by SCADA design engineers to **validate that the polling load does not saturate the PLC’s communication port** — a saturated port silently drops data, which is a leading cause of plant trip incidents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Scan Time of a SCADA Server

**Given:**
- A SCADA server is configured with **$N = 1200$** tags.
- The driver and protocol overhead require **$t_{per\,tag} = 25\ \text{ms}$** per tag poll (Modbus TCP over 100 Mbps LAN, average round-trip).
- The display refresh engine introduces an additional **$t_{graph} = 400\ \text{ms}$** per HMI cycle.
- The alarm engine scans independently and adds **$t_{alarm} = 150\ \text{ms}$**.

**Step 1 — Identify the constituent sub-times of one full SCADA cycle.**

A complete SCADA cycle includes:
1. Polling of all tags → $T_{poll}$
2. Updating the in-memory tag database → $T_{update}$ (negligible, < 5 ms — included in $t_{per\,tag}$).
3. Repainting the HMI screen → $t_{graph}$
4. Scanning for new alarms → $t_{alarm}$

**Step 2 — Write the governing equation.**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{poll} + t_{graph} + t_{alarm}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $T_{poll}$ in terms of $N$ and $t_{per\,tag}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{poll} &= N \cdot t_{per\,tag} \\
         &= 1200 \cdot 25\ \text{ms} \\
         &= 30{,}000\ \text{ms} \\
         &= 30.0\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Add the graphical and alarm sub-times.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{scan} &= 30{,}000\ \text{ms} + 400\ \text{ms} + 150\ \text{ms} \\
         &= 30{,}550\ \text{ms} \\
         &= 30.55\ \text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Analyse the result.**

A scan time of **30.55 s** is *unacceptable* for a process plant. A typical *fast* SCADA system polls in **1–2 s**, while a *slow* historian may poll in 30 s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Fix:** Use **multi-threaded driver channels** (split 1200 tags into 4 parallel drivers of 300 tags each) so that the **effective** scan time becomes:
> $$T_{scan,\,eff} = \frac{30.55\ \text{s}}{4} \approx 7.64\ \text{s}$$
> Still slow — so in real systems, **data exception reporting** is enabled: tags that have not changed by more than the **deadband** (e.g., 0.5 %) are *not* sent, cutting the effective update load to **5–10 %** of $N$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: % Span Conversion for a 4-20 mA Signal

A level transmitter has a range of **0 to 5 m** and outputs **4–20 mA**. If the loop current measured by the SCADA analogue input card is **12.5 mA**, what is the **equivalent level in metres** and the **% span**?

**Step 1 — Recall the linear 4-20 mA conversion formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Span} &= \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $I = 12.5$ mA.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{Span} &= \dfrac{12.5 - 4}{16} \times 100 \\
               &= \dfrac{8.5}{16} \times 100 \\
               &= 53.125 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the % span to engineering units using the range $(L_{max} - L_{min}) = 5\ \text{m}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{actual} &= L_{min} + \dfrac{\% \text{Span}}{100} \cdot (L_{max} - L_{min}) \\
           &= 0 + \dfrac{53.125}{100} \cdot 5 \\
           &= 2.656\ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check.**

- At $I = 4$ mA → $L = 0$ m ✓
- At $I = 20$ mA → $L = 5$ m ✓
- At $I = 12$ mA → $L = 2.5$ m (50 % span) ✓ (matches linear interpolation)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — KTU Frequently-Asked Scenarios

| # | Scenario Asked in KTU | Key Engineering Insight | Mark Weightage |
|---|------------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | *“How is SCADA different from a DCS?”* | SCADA = geographically distributed, event-driven, open protocols; DCS = tightly integrated, plant-local, proprietary, fast closed-loop | 4–5 marks |
| 2 | *“What is exception reporting?”* | Field sends data only when change exceeds deadband; reduces bandwidth load by 80–95 % | 3 marks |
| 3 | *“Why is OPC UA preferred over OPC DA?”* | DA is COM/DCOM-based (Windows-only); UA is platform-independent, secure (TLS), and supports complex data types | 5 marks |
| 4 | *“What is the role of a Historian?”* | Time-series database archiving all tag values; uses compression algorithms like *swinging-door* or *boxcar* | 4 marks |
| 5 | *“Differentiate between polling and report-by-exception.”* | Polling = master asks every cycle; RBE = slave reports only on change | 3 marks |
| 6 | *“Why is time-stamping critical in SCADA?”* | Enables sequence-of-events (SOE) analysis post-trip; resolution < 1 ms in IEC 61850 | 3 marks |
| 7 | *“Explain the role of redundancy in SCADA.”* | Hot-standby servers, dual LANs, RAID disks; failover < 5 s | 4 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SCADA Functional Architecture — Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of data** through a SCADA system. It is the single most valuable diagram for a KTU board answer worth **8–10 marks**.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FL["FIELD LEVEL - Sensors Transmitters Actuators"] --> CONV["SIGNAL CONDITIONING - 4-20mA RTD TC Pulse"]
    CONV --> PLC["TIER 1 - PLC and RTU Controllers"]
    PLC --> DRV["COMMUNICATION DRIVER - Modbus OPC Profinet"]
    DRV --> DB["REAL TIME TAG DATABASE - RAM"]
    DB --> HMI["HMI MIMIC SCREENS - Trends Alarms Graphics"]
    DB --> ALM["ALARM AND EVENT ENGINE"]
    DB --> HIS["HISTORIAN - Long Term Storage"]
    DB --> SCR["SCRIPTING ENGINE - Logic and Math"]
    HMI --> OP["OPERATOR WORKSTATION"]
    ALM --> OP
    HIS --> RPT["REPORT GENERATOR"]
    SCR --> PLC
    DRV --> MES["TIER 3 - MES and ERP Northbound"]
    HMI --> SEC["SECURITY LAYER - RBAC LDAP"]
    ALM --> SEC
```

**Reading the diagram:** Data originates in the **field**, is digitised by the **PLC**, pushed by the **driver** into the **tag database**, and from there the three parallel branches (HMI / Alarm / Historian) feed the **operator**. A **bi-directional arrow** between the script engine and the PLC indicates *write-back* control (e.g., setpoint dispatch).

## 4.2 HMI Screen Design Flow (Mimic Creation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 - Insert Static Background Pumps Pipes Tanks"] --> S2["Step 2 - Bind Dynamic Objects to Tags"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 - Configure Animations Level Colour Rotation"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 - Attach Alarms Limits and Priorities"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 - Validate With PLC Online Simulation"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 - Publish to Runtime and Test"]
```

## 4.3 Alarm Lifecycle State Machine (ISA 18.2 Compliant)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NORMAL
    NORMAL --> UNACK_ACTIVE: PV crosses limit
    UNACK_ACTIVE --> ACK_ACTIVE: Operator acknowledges
    ACK_ACTIVE --> ACK_CLEAR: PV returns to normal
    UNACK_ACTIVE --> UNACK_CLEAR: PV returns to normal
    ACK_ACTIVE --> NORMAL: Operator clears
    ACK_CLEAR --> NORMAL: After dwell time
    UNACK_CLEAR --> NORMAL: After dwell time
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always label alarms using the **ISA 18.2 hierarchy** — `LAH` (Level Alarm High), `LAL` (Low), `LAHH` (High-High), `LALL` (Low-Low). High-High alarms trigger **safety interlocks**, not just displays.

## 4.4 Modbus Master-Slave Transaction Timing Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as SCADA Master
    participant S as PLC Slave
    M->>S: Function 03 - Read Holding Registers (T+0 ms)
    Note over S: Slave decodes and reads internal data
    S-->>M: Response with N registers (T+25 ms)
    Note over M: Master updates tag DB and redraws HMI
    M->>S: Next slave request (T+30 ms)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define SCADA. List any four major functions of a SCADA system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** is a computer-based industrial control system that centrally monitors and controls geographically dispersed field assets using a combination of RTUs/PLCs, communication networks, and HMIs.

**Four major functions:**

1. **Data Acquisition** — Real-time polling of field instruments (temperature, pressure, flow, level).
2. **Supervisory Control** — Operator-issued setpoint / start / stop commands back to the controller.
3. **Alarm & Event Management** — Detection, prioritisation, acknowledgement, and archiving of abnormal process conditions.
4. **Historical Trending & Reporting** — Long-term storage of process data for performance analysis, audit, and regulatory compliance.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for 4 functions, ½ mark each)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between **Polling** and **Report-by-Exception (RBE)** in SCADA communication.*

| Aspect | Polling | Report-by-Exception |
|--------|---------|---------------------|
| **Direction** | Master-driven | Slave-driven |
| **Trigger** | Cyclic timer | Change beyond deadband |
| **Bandwidth** | High (all tags) | Low (only changed tags) |
| **Latency for change** | Up to one poll cycle | Near-instantaneous |
| **Use case** | Small networks, critical tags | Large SCADA, > 5000 tags |

*(2 marks for table + 1 mark for example: e.g., RBE used in DNP3/IEC 60870 for substation automation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** confuse SCADA with a PLC — SCADA *supervises*, PLC *controls*.
> - **Do not** write “SCADA is a software” — it is an **integrated system** of hardware, software, and networks.
> - **Do not** forget the **standards** (ISA-95, IEC 60870, IEC 61850, IEEE C37.90) — dropping these names fetches 1–2 extra marks.
> - **Do not** describe **DCS** when the question is about **SCADA** — examiners will deduct marks.
> - **Always** draw a **labelled block diagram** — diagrams carry 30–40 % of total marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a SCADA system**. Describe the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Modbus TCP/IP protocol** with its **transaction format**, and describe how a SCADA master reads a holding register from a slave PLC. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Q-A (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

A SCADA system has a **three-tier functional architecture**, as illustrated in Section 4.1. The blocks and their roles are:

| Block | Role | Marks |
|-------|------|-------|
| **Field I/O** | Sensors, transmitters, actuators generating 4-20 mA / RTD / pulse signals. | 1 |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Isolators, barriers, surge protection, intrinsic safety. | 0.5 |
| **PLC / RTU (Tier 1)** | Executes local closed-loop control, holds process values in registers. | 1 |
| **Communication Driver** | Modbus / OPC / DNP3 stack that polls the PLC and pushes to the tag DB. | 1 |
| **Real-Time Tag DB** | Central RAM-resident repository of all process values, addresses, and metadata. | 1 |
| **HMI / Mimic Engine** | Renders the operator screens, animations, trends, and tabular views. | 1 |
| **Alarm Engine** | Compares tags against limits; raises, prioritises, and routes alarms. | 0.5 |
| **Historian** | Long-term time-series storage; supports SQL/SwodDDL. | 0.5 |
| **Security Layer** | RBAC, audit log, two-factor authentication. | 0.5 |

*(5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for table of roles)*

---

#### Model Answer for Q-A (b) — Modbus TCP/IP (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Protocol Stack**
Modbus TCP/IP rides on **TCP port 502**. It is a **Client-Server** (formerly Master-Slave) protocol.

**Step 2 — ADU (Application Data Unit) Format**

$$
\text{MBAP} \ (7\ \text{bytes}) \; + \; \text{Function Code} \ (1\ \text{byte}) \; + \; \text{Data} \ (N\ \text{bytes})
$$

The **MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol)** header contains:

| Field | Size | Purpose |
|-------|------|---------|
| Transaction ID | 2 bytes | Matches request/response |
| Protocol ID | 2 bytes | Always 0x0000 for Modbus |
| Length | 2 bytes | Remaining byte count |
| Unit ID | 1 byte | Slave address (1–247) |

**Step 3 — Function Code 03 (Read Holding Registers)**

- **Request:** `00 01 00 00 00 06 01 03 00 00 00 0A`
  - `00 01` = Tx ID, `00 00` = Protocol ID, `00 06` = Length, `01` = Slave 1
  - `03` = Function Code, `00 00` = Start address, `00 0A` = Read 10 registers

- **Response:** `00 01 00 00 00 17 01 03 14 00 C8 01 2C 02 58 03 84 …`
  - First **10 registers** returned, each 2 bytes = 20 bytes of data.

**Step 4 — SCADA Configuration**
- In the SCADA tag database, an analogue tag `PV_Tank_101` is mapped to **slave 1, function 03, address 40001**.
- The driver executes the request every **$t_{poll} = 1\ \text{s}$** and stores the response in the tag's memory.
- The HMI animates a level symbol based on the live tag value.

*(3 marks for stack + format + function code explanation, 2 marks for the request/response example, 2 marks for SCADA tag binding flow)*

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style Model]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** as applied to a SCADA system, and explain why **layered separation of concerns** is critical for **cybersecurity**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **design procedure for building a SCADA mimic screen** in a software such as *InduSoft Web Studio* or *Ignition*. List the **configuration steps** and the **best practices** for an industrial mimic. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer for Q-B (a) — ISA-95 / Purdue Model (7 marks)

The **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model** is the de-facto industry standard for partitioning an industrial automation network into **five levels** (Levels 0 to 4), each with distinct **security, latency, and access policies**.

| Level | Name | Examples | Cybersecurity Zone |
|-------|------|----------|---------------------|
| **L0** | Physical Process | Sensors, motors, valves | Field Zone |
| **L1** | Basic Control | PLC, RTU, DCS Controller | Control Zone |
| **L2** | Supervisory | SCADA, HMI, Engineering Station | Supervisory Zone |
| **L3** | Operations | MES, historian, performance KPIs | Operations Zone |
| **L4 / L5** | Enterprise / Internet | ERP, cloud, email, vendor VPN | Enterprise Zone |

**Why layered separation matters:**

1. **Containment of Cyber Incidents** — A breach at L4 (e.g., a phishing email) cannot directly reach L1 (PLC) if a *conduit firewall* and *DMZ* sit between L2 and L3, as required by **IEC 62443-3-3**.
2. **Deterministic Real-Time Performance** — L1 traffic (Modbus TCP, Profinet) must be **untouched** by L3/L4 video calls or ERP syncs. The Purdue model enforces dedicated **process-control LANs**.
3. **Differential Authorisation** — Operators at L2 have *display-only* rights; engineers at L2.5 have *download-to-PLC* rights; corporate at L4 has *no plant control* rights.
4. **Audit & Traceability** — Each level retains its own audit log, enabling forensic analysis of an incident.
5. **Compliance** — Standards like **NERC-CIP** (power), **IEC 62443** (process), and **TSA Pipeline Directive** mandate Purdue-style separation.

*(2 marks for diagram of levels, 3 marks for the table, 2 marks for cybersecurity reasoning)*

---

#### Model Answer for Q-B (b) — SCADA Mimic Design (7 marks)

**Configuration Steps (with industry standard practice):**

1. **Project Creation** — Open InduSoft / Ignition, create a new project, name it (e.g., *Boiler_SCADA_v1.0*), and set the resolution to **1920 × 1080** for modern 16:9 panels.
2. **Driver / Channel Configuration** — Add the *Modbus TCP* driver, set the PLC IP (e.g., `192.168.1.10`) and port `502`, and define the *polling rate* (e.g., **500 ms**).
3. **Tag Database Definition** — Create main tags, e.g.:
   - `LT_101` (Level Transmitter) — analogue, address 400001, range 0–5 m.
   - `P_101` (Pump status) — digital, address 000001.
4. **Mimic Screen Layout** — Use the *Symbol Factory* library to drag pumps, tanks, and pipes onto the canvas.
5. **Animation Binding** — Click the tank level indicator → bind its *fill height* property to `LT_101 / SP`; bind the pump rotation to `P_101` (0 = stopped, 1 = running).
6. **Alarm Configuration** — Define alarm limits: `LT_101` HH = 4.5 m, H = 4.0 m, L = 0.5 m, LL = 0.2 m. Assign priority (1 = Highest).
7. **Trends** — Add a *Pen Trend* widget, add `LT_101` and `FT_102` as pens, set time axis to 30 min, 1 hour, 8 hour.
8. **Script Logic (optional)** — Write a *start-up* script that disables the pump if `LT_101` < 0.5 m.
9. **Validation** — Switch the driver to *Simulation Mode*, force tag values, and confirm the mimic updates.
10. **Runtime Deployment** — Publish to the SCADA runtime, launch the operator client, and conduct a **Factory Acceptance Test (FAT)** before site deployment.

**Best Practices:**

- Use **standardised colour coding** (green = running, red = stopped, amber = fault, grey = healthy).
- Provide a **navigation bar** at the top of every screen (Overview → Area 1 → Area 2 → Trends → Alarms).
- Keep **screen depth ≤ 3 levels** to avoid operator disorientation.
- Use **deadband filtering** on tank-level animations to prevent jitter.
- Always include a **log-off button** and **user-ID stamp** on every screen for audit.
- **Avoid embedding passwords** in the project file — use the central user manager.

*(3 marks for the 10 steps, 2 marks for the colour/navigation best practices, 2 marks for security/audit best practices)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Box Before the Exam**

- ☐ SCADA = **Supervisory Control & Data Acquisition**; *supervises* — does not control directly.
- ☐ Three-tier architecture: **Field → Controller → Supervisory (HMI)**.
- ☐ Purdue Model = **ISA-95, Levels 0–4**; mandatory in cyber-security answers.
- ☐ Key protocols: **Modbus RTU/TCP, OPC DA/UA, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104, IEC 61850, Profinet, MQTT**.
- ☐ **Modbus TCP uses port 502**; **OPC UA uses port 4840**.
- ☐ **Function Code 03** = Read Holding Registers; **Function Code 06** = Write Single Register; **Function Code 16** = Write Multiple Registers.
- ☐ **4-20 mA conversion**: $\% \text{Span} = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100$.
- ☐ **Scan time formula**: $T_{scan} = N \cdot t_{per\,tag}$ (parallel channels cut this by $k$, where $k$ = number of channels).
- ☐ **Alarm naming**: LAH / LAL / LAHH / LALL per ISA 18.2.
- ☐ **Historian** = long-term compressed time-series DB; algorithms: *Boxcar, Swinging-Door, Turning-Point*.
- ☐ **Polling vs RBE**: Polling = cyclic; RBE = change-triggered (bandwidth saver).
- ☐ **Redundancy**: Hot-standby servers, dual LANs, RAID-1/5/6, failover < 5 s.
- ☐ **Cybersecurity standards**: **IEC 62443**, **NERC-CIP**, **NIST SP 800-82**.
- ☐ **Viva favourites**:
  - *“What happens if the SCADA server crashes?”* → PLC continues running on its own logic; HMI shows *“Server Disconnected”*; redundant server takes over.
  - *“What is the difference between SCADA and HMI?”* → HMI is the *display layer* of SCADA; SCADA = HMI + tag DB + driver + historian.
  - *“Why is OPC UA replacing OPC DA?”* → Platform-independent, secure (TLS/encryption), supports complex types, can run over MQTT.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automation Studio Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCRUL507 - AUTOMATION LABORATORY
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Automation Studio Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Automation Studio** is an integrated engineering environment (IEE) developed by **B&R Industrial Automation** (Bernecker + Rainer Industrie-Elektronik GmbH, Austria) for the design, programming, simulation, commissioning, and diagnostics of complex automation systems. It is the de facto standard software platform used in industrial automation labs aligned with KTU's PCRUL507 syllabus for configuring Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs), motion control systems, and industrial communication networks.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Automation Studio is defined as:
> A unified programming and simulation suite conforming to **IEC 61131-3** standards, supporting graphical and textual programming languages for industrial automation, real-time control logic development, and HMI visualization within a single integrated development environment (IDE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Automation Studio is the official software platform adopted by the Department of Electronics & Instrumentation / Electrical & Electronics Engineering for PCRUL507 – Automation Laboratory. Students are expected to demonstrate proficiency in project creation, ladder logic implementation, and simulation of sequential control processes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **chess grandmaster** preparing for a tournament. Before making any real move on the actual board, you simulate every possible combination on a virtual chess engine. You test strategies, anticipate opponent responses, and refine your tactics — all without touching a single physical piece.

**Automation Studio is the "virtual chessboard" of industrial automation.**

- The **real machine** (conveyor belt, robotic arm, bottling plant) is your physical chessboard.
- The **PLC** is the grandmaster executing moves.
- **Automation Studio** is the simulator where you write, test, debug, and visualize every move (control logic) before it ever reaches the real machine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Recap:** Automation Studio = Integrated Development Environment (IDE) + Simulator + Commissioning Tool for B&R PLCs and industrial automation systems.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

The following standardized industrial terms are central to Automation Studio:

| Term | Meaning | Unit |
|------|---------|------|
| **Cycle Time (t<sub>c</sub>)** | Time taken to complete one scan of the PLC program | milliseconds (**ms**) |
| **I/O Update Time** | Time to refresh sensor and actuator values | **ms** |
| **Task Class** | Priority level of a program in real-time execution | Integer (0–15 typical) |
| **Watchdog Time** | Maximum allowed execution time before fault | **ms** |
| **Program Organization Unit (POU)** | A logical code block (program, function block, function) | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of Automation Studio

Automation Studio follows a **layered engineering model** that mirrors the actual plant architecture. Each layer represents a different engineering discipline, all integrated into a single project file.

#### Layer 1: Physical Layer (Hardware Configuration)
- Defines the **PLC rack topology** (X20 series, APC, etc.)
- Maps real-world **I/O modules** (digital input DI, digital output DO, analog AI, AO)
- Configures **fieldbus networks** (POWERLINK, CANopen, Profibus, EtherCAT)

#### Layer 2: Logical Layer (Program Structure)
- Contains **Programs, Functions, and Function Blocks** conforming to IEC 61131-3
- Defines **global variables, local variables, and I/O mapping**
- Supports **task configuration** for cyclic, event-driven, and freewheeling tasks

#### Layer 3: Communication Layer
- Configures industrial protocols (POWERLINK real-time Ethernet)
- Sets up **HMI screens**, SCADA tags, and OPC-UA servers
- Manages **client-server communication** between PLCs

#### Layer 4: Visualization Layer
- Built-in **HMI editor** for operator panel design
- **VC (Visual Components)** for trend curves, alarms, and recipe management
- **Web-based visualization** support

### 2.2 IEC 61131-3 Programming Languages Supported

Automation Studio supports **all five** languages defined by the IEC 61131-3 standard:

1. **Ladder Diagram (LD)** — Graphical, relay-logic-based, ideal for Boolean control
2. **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** — Graphical, signal-flow-based, ideal for analog/continuous control
3. **Structured Text (ST)** — Textual, Pascal-like, ideal for complex math and data handling
4. **Instruction List (IL)** — Textual, assembly-like, low-level operations
5. **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — Graphical, state-based, ideal for sequential processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Focus:** Ladder Diagram (LD) and Structured Text (ST) are the **two most-tested languages** in PCRUL507 lab examinations.

### 2.3 The 'Why' Behind the Software Design

- **Why IEC 61131-3?** → Vendor-neutral, internationally standardized, allows portability of code across PLC brands.
- **Why integrated simulation?** → Reduces commissioning time by ~**40–60%**, prevents costly plant shutdowns during testing.
- **Why object-oriented extensions?** → Modern automation systems require reusable, modular code (B&R supports OOP in ST via classes, methods, inheritance).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below summarizes the key timing, performance, and control formulas used in Automation Studio experiments and examinations.

> **Notation Convention:** All formulas use standard IEC variable naming. `T` denotes a task cycle, `t<sub>r</sub>` denotes response time, and `t<sub>w</sub>` denotes the watchdog time.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Task Cycle Time** | $T_c = T_{period} - t_{overhead}$ | Effective cycle time after system overhead |
| 2 | **PLC Response Time** | $t_r = t_{input} + t_{scan} + t_{output}$ | Total I/O response latency |
| 3 | **Scan Time Budget** | $t_{scan} \leq \alpha \cdot t_{w}$ where $\alpha = 0.8$ | Must not exceed 80% of watchdog |
| 4 | **Throughput (conveyor)** | $v = \dfrac{L}{T_c}$ | Items processed per unit time |
| 5 | **Counter Frequency Limit** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot T_c}$ | Nyquist limit for digital counters |
| 6 | **PID Output** | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Continuous-time PID controller |
| 7 | **Discrete PID (velocity form)** | $\Delta u_k = K_p (e_k - e_{k-1}) + K_i \cdot e_k + K_d (e_k - 2e_{k-1} + e_{k-2})$ | Anti-windup discrete form |
| 8 | **POU Memory Footprint** | $M_{POU} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{size}(v_i) + M_{code}$ | Total memory per program unit |
| 9 | **Analog Resolution** | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | Voltage per LSB of an n-bit ADC |
| 10 | **Debounce Time** | $t_{db} \geq n \cdot T_c$ | Where n = scan cycles to confirm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** When writing in the KTU answer sheet, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$ instead of the pipe character `$\vert x \vert$` within tables to avoid markdown syntax conflicts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Automation Studio-trained engineers find employment in:
- **Automotive manufacturing** (BMW, Mercedes-Benz plant lines)
- **Food & beverage** (Coca-Cola bottling, Nestlé packaging)
- **Pharmaceutical** (FDA-validated batch process control)
- **Power plants** (turbine control, grid synchronization)
- **Water treatment** (SCADA-based pumping stations)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case PLC Scan Time

In a KTU examination or lab viva, you may be asked to justify the choice of task cycle time. The derivation below establishes the relationship between **task period, program execution time, and watchdog safety margin**.

> **Given:** A cyclic task with period $T_p$, system overhead $t_{oh}$, and a program containing $n$ POUs each with average execution time $t_{i}$.

#### Step 1: Total Program Execution Time

We sum the execution times of all program organization units (POUs) in the task:

$$
T_{exec} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + \cdots + t_n
$$

**Explanation:** Each POU (program, function block, function) consumes CPU time during one scan. The total is the arithmetic sum of individual runtimes.

#### Step 2: Add Operating System Overhead

The real-time OS (ARemb) adds a fixed overhead for task switching, I/O updates, and communication:

$$
T_{total} = T_{exec} + t_{oh}
$$

**Explanation:** The overhead is typically **0.5 ms to 2 ms** depending on the controller (e.g., X20CP3584 has ~0.8 ms typical).

#### Step 3: Apply the Watchdog Safety Rule

The watchdog timer $t_w$ protects the system from runaway loops. Industry standard requires the total scan time to be at most **80%** of the watchdog:

$$
T_{total} \leq 0.8 \cdot t_w
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i + t_{oh} \leq 0.8 \cdot t_w
$$

#### Step 4: Solve for the Minimum Required Task Period

To guarantee safety, choose the task period as:

$$
T_p \geq T_{total} + t_{margin}
$$

where the margin $t_{margin} \geq 0.2 \cdot t_w$ absorbs jitter and interrupt latency.

#### Step 5: Final Consolidated Expression

$$
\boxed{\; T_p \geq \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i + t_{oh} + 0.2 \cdot t_w \;}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** If your program has 10 POUs each taking 0.3 ms, overhead is 1 ms, and watchdog is 10 ms, then:

$$
T_p \geq (10 \times 0.3) + 1 + 0.2(10) = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

Choose $T_p = 10$ ms (round up to standard values: 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 ms).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Automation Studio Workflow

The following matrix maps each engineering phase to the corresponding Automation Studio tool, deliverable, and KTU learning outcome.

| Engineering Phase | Automation Studio Tool | Deliverable | CO Mapping | Bloom's Level |
